That's an interesting point. Trio uses `await` to help users keep track of cancel+schedule points, but that means it can't be used to keep track of which operations require 'trio context'. Curio uses `await` to help users keep track of which operations require 'curio context', but that means it can't be used to keep track of cancel+schedule points. On the whole I think trio's choice is probably correct, because (a) if you look at existing async programs then the majority of code is inside async context and not doing clever things like jumping back and forth between threads, so most of the time users don't need to keep track of this distinction, (b) when people get confused about cancel/schedule points it leads to subtle latent bugs, while trying to call a trio function outside of trio context immediately [raises an exception](https://github.com/python-trio/trio/blob/72dba90e31604c083a177978c40c4dd8570aee21/trio/_core/_run.py#L1529), and (c) constructors may depend on being called from trio context but can't be marked async.

But it is a real trade-off, with advantages and disadvantages either way.

> the `examples/requestio` example

Ha, I hadn't seen this. That's extremely cute :-). So does that mean that if your call stack goes from curio → thread → back into curio, then the cancellation system tracks that all the way through? That's impressive, and interesting...

I want to just note for the record that when we say "priorities" here we're talking specifically about are "strict" priorities, like Linux's real-time priorities, *not* the priorities that you get with WFQ or unix "nice"-style priorities ([see here for more discussion of the distinction](https://github.com/dabeaz/curio/pull/115#issuecomment-262421366)). WFQ prioritizes tasks on the run queue, but uses FIFO (or whatever) for wait queues.

As a general principle, I really don't think we should be making decisions about how the code should look *now* based on similarity to how it might look if we later add some other feature. If we decide to add support for priorities later, then implementing that feature will necessarily involve surgery to curio's guts anyway; we can always change the waitqueue implementation as part of that. And at that point we'll have a much better idea of what kind of waitqueue actually makes sense for the specific priority design we end up with. And if we don't decide to support strict priorities, then we've *really* wasted our time worrying about it now. (This general design principle is what agile folks call "YAGNI".) So my preference would be to implement the best FIFO waitqueue for now and let the future take care of itself.

That said, it is an interesting intellectual question how to best implement a wait queue that supports multiple priorities :-). So here's a review of some options. (Spoiler: I think maybe I like the last one best?)

# heapq

One option would be a heapq + a counter that increments for each task queued, and is used to guarantee FIFO ordering within a priority level:
```python
class HeapqBasedPriorityWaitingQueue:
    def __init__(self):
        self._tasks = []
        self._counter = itertools.count()
    def append(self, task):
        heapq.heappush(self._tasks, (task.priority, next(self._counter), task))
    # etc.
```
An interesting feature of this data structure is that it potentially supports *mutating* a task's priority without it losing its place in line; whether that matters depends on whether that's a feature our hypothetical priority API wants to support, and also I guess on the exact details of what the API looks like. (E.g. you might only want to disallow arbitrary priority mutations but still support priority inheritance; priority inheritance is a little simpler though because priority changes are monotonic, which puts a bound on how many times its priority can change while its in a wait queue.)

But this doesn't support fast deletes, so we'd need "soft delete" support on top of it. How doable is this? Here's one strategy. Instead of enqueuing the actual task objects, we enqueue `(priority, counter, taskid, cookie)` tuples. The point of enqueueing `taskid`s instead of `Task`s is that this avoids having a defunct wait queue entry pin the `Task` object in memory. (NB that this also requires that `taskid`s have to be globally unique over the lifetime of the program. Which ours currently are, so that's fine. But e.g. if we started using `id(taskobject)` for `taskid` then this system would be broken.) The job of the `cookie` is to let us identify when an entry has been soft-deleted -- it's some arbitrary that's stored inside `Task` object itself, and which changes to a new globally unique (over the lifetime of this task) value every time the `Task` enters a wait queue. So something like:
```python
def append(self, task):
    task.cookie += 1
    heapq.heappush(self._tasks, (task.priority, next(self._counter), task.taskid, task.cookie))

def popleft(self):
    while True:
        (_, _, taskid, cookie) = heapq.heappop(self._tasks)
        if taskid not in kernel_task_table:
            continue
        task = kernel_task_table[taskid]
        if task.cookie != cookie:
            continue
        return

def cancel_wait(task):
    task.cookie += 1
```

However, this still has (at least one) edge case where it will blow up in a nasty way. Consider an HTTP/2 server with the following design:

* There's a `curio.Event` that is used to signal that the server is entering "graceful shutdown" mode (i.e. it should stop accepting new requests, but should finish up the existing ones)
* Each incoming HTTP/2 connection spawns a task whose job it is just to watch for this event being signaled (and if it is it will do stuff like send a message to the client telling it that it's not allowed to make any more requests).
* Most of the time, though, the connection completes without the event being signaled, so the watcher task just gets canceled.

With our heapq design so far, this combination of factors will lead to a situation where this `Event`'s waitqueue grows monotonically, forever, until the server is shut down or we run out of memory. (Rough estimate of the leak rate: it looks like each entry in the queue consumes about `1.__sizeof__() * 4 + (1, 2, 3, 4).__sizeof__() + 8 = 176` bytes of memory, so a server handling ~10 connections/second will leak ~150 MB/day.)

I can't see any way to solve this except to keep statistics on how many "soft deletes" are pending inside the waitqueue, and trigger a "hard delete" cycle if it grows to large. (The idea would be that this is still an improvement over the naive "hard delete" approach, because we can batch up a set of hard-deletes together to amortize their cost, so it's not an amortized O(n).) This is unfortunate, because the nicest thing about this design so far is the `delete` implementation being so simple; I guess this means that each `Task` actually needs to keep a reference to the queue its waiting on, and increment a counter, compare its value to the overall size of the waitqueue, etc. Something like:
```python
def cancel_wait(task):
    task.cookie += 1
    task.waitq._soft_deletes_pending += 1
    if task.waitq._soft_deletes_pending > 100 and 2 * task.waitq._soft_deletes_pending > len(task.waitq._tasks):
        task.waitq._process_hard_deletes()
    task.waitq = None
```
(plus `popleft` needs to decrement `_soft_deletes_pending` appropriately, etc.)

Overall it seems workable, but it's rather complex -- enough that I'd be nervous we've missed some more options -- and it may or may not be the fastest option.

# list-based approaches

The Linux kernel uses an interesting data structure for this -- it's basically a doubly-linked list, but with a second skiplist-like (or "skiplist-lite") index on top: http://lxr.free-electrons.com/source/include/linux/plist.h#L19

The idea is that at each moment the underlying list is a simple linear queue of tasks in the order that they should run, so high-priority tasks are in front of low-priority tasks. But you also have a second index list with one entry per priority, and each entry points to the first task with that priority in the underlying list. So when you want to insert a new task, you do a linear scan through the index list, and this lets you find the other tasks at the same priority so you can insert the new task immediately after them. This is O(k) where k is the number of distinct priorities used by tasks waiting on this queue, but that's not too bad, because on average k is small because most programs don't use many different priority levels, and in the worst case it's bounded to k <= 100 because the Linux RT scheduler is hard-coded to only support integer priorities between 1 and 99. If one wanted to support unbounded priorities, then the natural extension would be to use a real multi-layered skiplist, which would make inserts and deletes O(log k). This data structure is somehow the most "natural" one for this task, I think -- the operations that it makes easy/fast/natural are exactly the ones that we care about, without need for trickiness like soft delete. Of course, "naturalness" is not the only criterion :-).

# sorted container

A third approach would be to use a classic sorted container, the kind that supports O(log n) insertion, O(log n) deletion (given the key of the object to delete), and sorted iteration. [sortedcontainers](http://www.grantjenks.com/docs/sortedcontainers/) has a reputation as a wicked-fast pure-Python library for this kind of container structure. So something like:

```python
from sortedcollections import SortedList
class SortedListWaitQueue:
    def __init__(self):
        self._tasks = SortedList(key=lambda task: (task.priority, task.wait_ticket))
        self._counter = itertools.count()

    def append(self, task):
        task.waitqueue = self
        task.wait_ticket = next(self._counter)
        self._tasks.add(task)

    def popleft(self, task):
        return self._tasks.pop(0)

def cancel_wait(task):
    task.waitqueue._tasks.remove(task)
    task.waitqueue = task.wait_key = None

def reprioritize_task(task, new_priority):
    task.waitqueue._tasks.remove(task)
    task.priority = new_priority
    task.waitqueue._tasks.add(task)
```

Theoretically this is a bit wince-inducing; the data structure really isn't the one that's best fitted to the task, and O(log n) is worse than O(1). But the implementation is ridiculously simple and flexible, it avoids all the edge cases and worst cases, and I bet it's pretty fast in practice. (Sorta like `SortedList` actually, which if you look at its [internals](http://www.grantjenks.com/docs/sortedcontainers/implementation.html) is a beautiful example of how theoretical algorithm analysis doesn't always apply directly to the real world.)

Cmder (and by extension, clink) does keep a history file. Clink also provides tail and head commands. So, with some *really* janky trickery, I was able to make thefuck work on Windows with Cmder.

1. Run `clink set history_io 1` and restart your shell. By default clink only writes history on exit, this ensures it writes as commands are run.
2. Place this file somewhere in your path: https://gist.github.com/OfficerHalf/a9848df0a25706532f86dc8aaae56e4f
3. Run `alias fuck=thefuckcmder`

If you only want to use clink, not Cmder, you can replace all the `%CMDER_ROOT%` paths with `%LOCALAPPDATA%\clink\.history`

I'm sure there's a better way to do this, I'm just not sure what it is. This also does not bypass the Python encoding error mentioned [here](https://github.com/nvbn/thefuck/wiki/Shell-aliases#powershell).

Hi, 
Thanks for the bug report. 

I believe there is a collision between the two installations. 
Could you please try one of the following:
If you want to install via PPA:
Use `sudo apt install python3-s-tui`, and update `psutil` via `pip install psutil --upgrade`. A newer version of `psutil` is required for all the features to work. 

If you want to install via pip (which is usually the newest version):
First try completely uninstalling any installation of s-tui that was installed via PPA:
`sudo apt remove --purge python*-s-tui`
Possibly psutil as well:
`sudo apt remove --purge python*-psutil`
(The * if for both python2 and python3 versions) 
Try removing `/usr/bin/s-tui` and `/usr/local/bin/s-tui` if they were created and not removed by `apt remove`. 
Then install s-tui with `sudo pip install s-tui`. This will install the latest versions of `psutil` and `urwid` which are the only dependencies of `s-tui`.
Now try running `s-tui` in a new shell. 

Sorry if this sounds a bit complected, this is caused because the system is confused between the two installation methods and  the fact that only an older version of `psutil` is available in 16.04 based systems. Newer version (18.04) should have all the needed prerequisites. 

Please update if this worked out for you.

That's precisely the issue - Mopidy currently doesn't support Spotify's new endpoints. This is why Spotmop supports the option to use Mopidy's backend Spotify authentication, or the more capable _Authorization Code_ flow.

**Setup one** (no user-authenticated Spotify account):
* Unselect "Connect with Spotify" on initial setup
* Don't have a Spotify account, or don't want to use _Authorization Code_ flow
* All requests use the `Mopidy-Spotify` backend
* New Spotify endpoints and functionality that is not supported by `Mopidy-Spotify` is disabled. This includes _My Albums_, _My Tracks_ and _My Artists_ as they require additional authentication scopes.
* All other functionality remains (browsing playlists, playing music, browsing local library, etc)

**Setup two** (user-authenticated Spotify account):
* Select "Connect with Spotify" on initial setup (default)
* If existing setup, click "Login to Spotify" on the _Settings_ tab
* _Authorization Code_ flow uses popup and external server to complete authorization and provide tokens
* All Spotify requests use these tokens
* New Spotify functionality enabled (_My Albums_, _My Tracks_ and _My Artists_)


The reason Setup two is the default and preferred setup is that communicating directly with the Spotify Web API allows for a more streamlined and lightweight interface (only loading on demand). It also means the interface is less dependent on a powerful Mopidy server. This will continue to be the preference for Spotmop.

This means Spotmop works perfectly well on low-powered devices like the RaspberryPi. It also means you can continue to browse music while the Mopidy server is performing other requests (like batch loading a lot of tracks).

For one thing, that is canonical pyPDF code.  It is not canonical pdfrw code; you would use, for example, `page.Resources.XObjects = None` in pdfrw, and you would not necessarily open files and convert them to bytes before passing them to pdfrw (you can simply provide the filename).

But yes, removing in a naive fashion will leave dangling references inside the content stream.  pdfrw can parse into most content streams (it would need helpers for embedded images), but it is up to client code to ask it to parse into a content stream, and then to recognize and intelligently remove those references.

You might look at pdfminer; it understands PDF content more deeply.

1. It's not appropriate to import data from sqlite to mysql using sql file (generated by mysqldump or similar tools). Task db is not compatible in fields level. If you do want to transfer database backend, a script using pyspider.database API is needed.
2. You need restart pyspider after import the data.
3. In the design of pyspider, the parameters of a task is calculated when it's created (that means you can generate a UA for every task using any script code). Thus, after modified the script of project, there is no way except re-calcalute the parameters for each task. But, the context (response of the previous page  containing the task) is lost.
4. If you want to specify and change the user-agent, it's better using global config specify in command line `pyspider fetcher --user-agent=xxx`. And leave the user-agent parameter not modified in script. In this way, you can change the user-agent and take effect immediately.

@jaraco I've figured out how to implement this on Linux, and it is very Linux specific.  Perhaps adding this as another backend, or just deciding it is out of scope for the keyring project?

The solution is to use [keyctl](http://man7.org/linux/man-pages/man1/keyctl.1.html).  The keyrings are implemented by the kernel and non-persistent across reboots.  They can be accessed within the same process tree, by the same user, across a session and various other scopes.

This will add a key to the user-session (@us) scoped keyring and set a 1 hour timeout:

````
$ echo -n 'the secret' | keyctl padd user KEYNAME @us
... prints a keyid like 977746739 ....
$ keyctl timeout 977746739 3600
````

This is how you search for a key:
````
$ keyctl search @us user KEYNAME
... prints a keyid like 977746739 or return code non-zero and this
keyctl_search: Key has expired
````

This is how you get the contents of a key on stdout:
````
$ keyctl pipe 977746739
````

Original comment by: Maciej Sitarz

I'd like to avoid relying strictly on the module name, which as you've indicated would have implications on where one should store a keyring class.

A couple of things come to mind. You could use the module name + class name, which would yield:

```
kwallet.Keyring
kwallet.DBusKeyringKwallet4
SecretService.Keyring
fail.Keyring
...
```

Better would be for each class to present its own user-friendly name (and maybe description).

If the base class had this class property:

```

@ClassProperty
@classmethod
def name(cls):
    parent, sep, mod_name = cls.__module__.rpartition('.')
    mod_name = mod_name.replace('_', ' ')
    return ' '.join([mod_name, cls.__name__])
```

Then you could do:

```
names = [be.name for be in keyring.backend.get_all_keyring()]
```

And that would result in something like:

```
names == ['OS X Keyring', 'SecretService Keyring', 'kwallet Keyring', 'kwallet DBusKeyringKWallet4', ...]
```

And if any particular class doesn't present a suitable name, it can be overridden by setting `name` on the class.

It is not trivial though, because of the way the SCP protocol works: we request something (usually files or folders, potentially with wildcards) and then the server sends multiple commands back (which can be a file, or file metadata, or recursing in a subdirectory). Things like this are funny. On one hand, it's trivial to implement, and on the other hand we need to have it behave in a way that is obvious, logical, and intuitive.

I initially intentionally left the connection out of the timeout management, as I think it's not actually really expected to include it on a high level. For example, calling something that takes a defined about of time. Easy example is httpbin's /delay endpoint.

Even as it stands now, thanks to network io, if we call /delay/1 with a timeout of 1, it will timeout every single time. This isn't really black magic stuff though. Users know that network io is at play and can account for that. So for /delay/1 a timeout of 1.5 seconds may be reasonable for them, and set.

What is less obvious though, is that different servers have different concurrency capabilities and different `connection` header policies, and may be slow to allot them a socket or whatever. Now we have a total operation of say, 5 seconds to run the /delay/1 endpoint the first time, so it fails and they adjust to 5.5 seconds. Then the connection is pooled for them and the server uses `connection: keep-alive`, so all of their subsequent requests are waiting four seconds too long.

Ideally I'd like to avoid complexity, but I think in the end it may make sense to have an additional `connection_timeout` kwarg with a default, that people can go and fine tune if needs be.

How about a default of 60 seconds, half the the maximum (segment lifetime) time a packet can float around the interwebs?

https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Maximum_segment_lifetime

since this is a brainstorming section for an even larger fishnet: how about utilizing all the mobile devices out there?

arm cpus are not as powerful as x86/amd64. Plus battery drain is another huge issue with them. so here's my proposal:
A "mobile-fishnet" API endpoint would not give full games to analyze rather than just single positions. The server's responsibility would be to distribute them among the requesting devices and reconstruct the pgn afterwards. 
as for the mobile devices: the logic could be embedded within the lichess mobile application, since it's already wide spread. Opening the app would trigger (with some reasonable delay) a request for a new position or two to analyze, do the job and be done with it until the app is opened again. Since nobody likes battery hogs further restrictions could be put in place - eg: only when battery status is > 50% or loading. furthermore the user should be given a opt-in (or opt-out) possibility.

I don't know the numbers, if there're sufficient mobile clients out there, how often they open/close the app per day, etc. - but given the popularity of lichess I like to think this could work. Just think about all the unused computing power people are carrying in their pockets and handbags ;)

You are mistaken as to what those additional characteristics are. The uuids that begin with 000028xx are characteristic "declarations" as seen here: [gatt declaration spec](https://www.bluetooth.com/specifications/gatt/declarations), and the uuids that begin with 000029xx are descriptors as seen here: [gatt descriptor spec](https://www.bluetooth.com/specifications/gatt/descriptors). They are not the characteristics itself, but describe details about one of the characteristics.

Hi

The answer is in the error itself: `[...] can only be used with threads started with QThread`. Hotkeys in `keyboard` are processed asynchronously, which means that your `mainDialog.show` callback is invoked from a separate thread. Qt is a bit brittle in this aspect and doesn't allow a UI call from a non-UI thread (i.e. should inherit `QThread` instead of Python's `threading.Thread`).

This is not a `keyboard` issue, but would have happened with any code that calls `mainDialog.show` from inside a `threading.Thread`.

The solution is to use Qt's **signals and slots**. This way you can emit a signal from the keyboard handler, and the slot will call the desired method from inside the main thread.

I'm closing the issue, but if you still fail to get your code to work and believe `keyboard` is to blame, or have any suggestions that could be implemented on this side, feel free to reopen the issue.

@craffel I ran into a snag in the interpolation when `len(time_centers)=1`. 

Using `kind='nearest'`:
```
gram_interpolator = interp1d(
    time_centers, gram[n, :],
    kind='linear' if len(time_centers) > 1 else 'nearest',
    bounds_error=False, fill_value=0.0)
```
it fails because when `kind='nearest'` it expects `time_centers` and `gram[n, :]` to have more than 1 point.

Using `kind='zero'`:
```
gram_interpolator = interp1d(
    time_centers, gram[n, :],
    kind='linear' if len(time_centers) > 1 else 'zero',
    bounds_error=False, fill_value=0.0)
```
The interpolator always returns zero -- for example:
```
f = interp1d([1], [3], kind='zero', bounds_error=False, fill_value=0.0)
f(-1)
>> array(0.0)
f(0)
>> array(0.0)
f(1)
>> array(2.1904428788e-314)
f(2)
>> array(0.0)
```

What do you want the expected behavior to be? For a length `n` output, do you want a the same set of pitches to be sustained, or something else?

P	R	F	P_nooff	R_nooff	F_nooff
----------------------------------------------

0.013	0.010	0.011	0.043	0.032	0.037

0.007	0.004	0.005	0.011	0.009	0.010	(mean diff)

All close 1e-1: True

Which actually brings the results back down quite a bit. So the differences to be document are:

1. We use graph matching instead of greedy matching: this brings the results up
2. We don't round onset and offset times. This brings the results down a tad
3. We **do** apply a minimum offset_tolerance of 0.05 (since it's in the MIREX description and the paper), which again brings our results up compared to the MIREX results.

For anyone who follows, I have set up a **simple bot program**, monitored by **[supervisord](http://supervisord.org/)** to capture any erroneous output, hopefully to get some clues on this. The setup is as follows:

Machine: **Raspberry Pi 2 Model B**
Raspbian: **Wheezy**
Python: **2.7**
telepot: **6.5**
Program: an **echo bot**, which only echoes the text message I have sent

```python
import sys
import time
import telepot

def handle(msg):
    msg_type, chat_type, chat_id = telepot.glance(msg)

    if msg_type != 'text':
        return

    text = msg['text']

    if chat_id != USER_ID:
        return

    bot.sendMessage(chat_id, text)

TOKEN = sys.argv[1]
USER_ID = long(sys.argv[2])

bot = telepot.Bot(TOKEN)
bot.notifyOnMessage(handle)
print 'Listening ...'

while 1:
    time.sleep(1)
```

In supervisord's program section, I have set `autorestart` to `false`, so the program would *not* get re-started after crashing.

As of now, it has been up for **48 hours** and is still receiving and echoing messages as expected. Except for the occasional `ConnectionError` encountered by `getUpdate` requests (which is normal), no other problems is apparent.

Stay tune for updates.

When you look at [RFC 5280](https://tools.ietf.org/html/rfc5280) which seems to be the current one, in [section 4.2.2.1](https://tools.ietf.org/html/rfc5280#section-4.2.2.1) you can find definition of the "Authority Information Access" field which defines "CA Issuers" field. This field points to the Issuer certificate of a given X.509 certificate, therefore providing a standard path which can be used to build a complete certificate chain.

Granted, when I checked the [CA/Browser Forum Baseline Requirements](https://cabforum.org/wp-content/uploads/CA-Browser-Forum-BR-1.3.7.pdf), I didn't find any specific information related to these fields. RFC doesn't specify them as required either. Still, it seems that these fields are present in all X.509 certificates in wide circulation and certificates issued by Let's Encrypt definitely have this field as well. You can check that by running command:
```
openssl x509 -in <path/to/certificate.pem> -text -noout
```
The specific code that `debops.pki` role uses to get the intermediate CA certificate URL, parse it, retrieve the certificate and handle DER->PEM conversion [can be found here](https://github.com/debops/ansible-pki/blob/master/files/usr/local/lib/pki/pki-realm#L1040-L1072).

One application that I know of that used to use intermediate certificates separately is Apache2 with the [SSLCertificateChainFile](https://httpd.apache.org/docs/current/mod/mod_ssl.html#sslcertificatechainfile) directive. It was obsoleted in Apache2 v2.4.8, so the support for intermediate certs improved, but there might still be others out there. Due to this I want to keep supporting that option if the need arises.

Always more room for more clients :) The goal of this project is minimalization.

Sysadmins should be able to ssh into their box, highlight the 200 lines of this script, paste it into a file, run it, and set it as their cron job. Boom free, automated certs. No installing dependencies or setup.py required. I want sysadmins to be able to read this script and understand what it's doing in 10 minutes or less, then start using it without having to do anything else.

Thanks @dpkp for the help!

One issue here could be that by default the async producer only waits up to 30 secs to complete pending message production (`ASYNC_STOP_TIMEOUT_SECS`). If you set `batch_send_every_t` to 60 seconds without also increasing this timeout then you may have producer threads that have messages left in queue when shutdown.

If you have logs you can share, I may be able to help debug more closely.

This is a design choose and the only way to avoid collisions with browsepy actions such as 'open', 'download', 'remove' and so on.

Not by default, no. All the hashing is done by Django's [ManifestStaticFilesStorage](https://docs.djangoproject.com/en/1.9/ref/contrib/staticfiles/#manifeststaticfilesstorage) class and that doesn't generated hashed filenames when `DEBUG=True`. WhiteNoise will still serve any hashed files if they're requested, it's just that the storage backend won't generated the right URLs in development.
Plus if WhiteNoise ever was merged into Django, I imagine the compression parts might not be accepted into core, so need to be standalone anyway.

Your code is very nice and concise, and surprisingly straightforward. Some (hopefully) minor comments:

1. Please remove the annotation comments which suppress warnings in your particular IDE or linter.
2. Please adapt to the naming conventions in this project which aren't entirely PEP 8. To be specific: variables and function parameters are mixedCase not lower_case_with_underscores.
3. Please replace the default value of `ciphers` by a constant (defined in the `ssl` module?).
4. Please add examples for the usage of both `sync` and `async` client. This would also make it easier to understand to SSL noobs (like myself) why the `async` code supports SSL without modifications; it apparently works via the URI and `twisted.internet.endpoints.clientFromString`, but it doesn't seem to make use of `stompest.config.SSLConfig`, so the latter seems to be specific to the `sync` client and should therefore reside in a module inside `stompest.sync` not `stompest`. It would be interesting for SSL noobs (like myself) to understand inhowfar the `sync` and the `async` versions differ in terms flexibility of SSL parameters.

Looking at the pictures, it almost certainly _looks like_ something else is emitting data on the same SPI channel. 

The fact that you see the warning

```
/usr/local/lib/python2.7/dist-packages/luma/core/interface/serial.py:164: RuntimeWarning: This channel is already in use, continuing anyway.  Use GPIO.setwarnings(False) to disable warnings.
  self._gpio.setup(pin, self._gpio.OUT)
```

indicates that there is another program sharing at least one GPIO pin.

Things I would look at to try and isolate the problem:
* Stop all non-standard scripts that you have running
* Change the CS (Chip select) to CE1 (P01-26) and then run the demo with `--spi-device=1`
* Burn a new raspian image on a spare SD card, boot up a clean system & install the libs, and retest

The stumbling block is UDP and that two applications running on the same machine can't have the same socket open.  One machine will have port 47808 and the other will have 47809, and it's easy to send unicast packets, but they are in a different broadcast domain.

Are you familiar with BACnet BBMD's?  You could have one application (A) running as a BBMD (BACnet Broadcast Management Device) and then have the other one (B) register as a foreign device.  Then all of the broadcast messages B wants to send are unicast to the BBMD as `distributeBroadcastToNetwork` messages and the BBMD will rebroadcast it.

Another option is to run some virtualization software (I use [VirtualBox](https://www.virtualbox.org/)) and run the applications on two different virtual machines.  And another option is to build a virtual network from the components in the `vlan` module and combine the applications into one program.

If you have a working implementation of a shortener service, you can create a shortener instance, pass the URL to that, and replace the long URL with the short URL in the status string, and then post the modified string to Api.PostUpdate():

```python
import re

from twitter import Api
from twitter.twitter_utils import URL_REGEXP
from shorten_url import ShortenURL

CONSUMER_KEY = 'WWWWWWW'
CONSUMER_SECRET = 'XXXXXXXXXXX'
ACCESS_TOKEN = 'YYYYYYYYYYY'
ACCESS_TOKEN_SECRET = 'ZZZZZZZZZ'


def PostStatusWithShortenedURL(status):
    shortener = ShortenURL()
    api = Api(CONSUMER_KEY,
              CONSUMER_SECRET,
              ACCESS_TOKEN,
              ACCESS_TOKEN_SECRET)

    urls = re.findall(URL_REGEXP, status)

    for url in urls:
        status = status.replace(url, shortener.Shorten(url), 1)

    api.PostUpdate(status)


if __name__ == '__main__':

What do you mean by "complicates things"? The way I see it is that there are three clear, separate things: (1) A representation of a service (2) the state of the service and (3) a thing which manipulates the state of the service as a result of interactions with the outside world.

Currently, the Accessory is both the representation of a service and its state and the driver is the thing that manipulates. What this change proposes is to move the state from the accessory to the driver, which is totally fine, although ideally we should separate it. However, by forcing the accessory to have a driver, we essentially need to have all three things created to get functionality even from only one of them. For example, to test the Accessory I have to have a driver. I don't see this is as bringing any benefits. Right now I can have and Accessory and a Driver separately, potentially reusing and substituting whatever I want individually.

 * `child_message@2` and `child_action@3,1` are ordered children of `root_action@1`
 * `child_action@3,2` is a child of `child_action@3,1`
 * `root_action@4` is a sibling of `root_action@1`

So if one were to describe the structure using `task_level` alone to produce a tree as per your description this would make the most sense to me:
 * `root_action@1` remains as is (the first node)
 * `child_message@2` becomes `child_message@1,1` (the first child of the first node)
 * `child_action@3,1` becomes `child_action@1,2` (the second child of the first node)
 * `child_action@3,2` becomes `child_action@1,2,1` (the first child of the second child of the first node)
 * `root_action@4` becomes `child_action@2` (the second node)

The fetch api's `Response.prototype.body` is a ReadableStream not a File, or Blob.
The saveAs method don't accept a stream.

You would have to do: 
```js
fileModel.fetch(url)
  .then(function(response) {
    return response.blob();
  })
  .then(function(blob) {
    fileSaver.saveAs(blob, file.filename);
  })
```

And the reason why it dosn't throw a error is cuz you wrapped it in a promise.
you would have to do `...).catch(console.log)` to see the error
 

---

PS. use arrow fn
```js
fileModel.fetch(url)
  .then(response => response.blob())
  .then(blob => fileSaver.saveAs(blob, file.filename))
```

Referring this attribute as `category` will be ok.

> Perhaps the rule parser should parse out this all caps data as "category" and not group - keeping group as the filename.

I think that `category` is just the second uppercase word of the `msg, considering also the first word or the third would cause some issue. IMO the category should enrich the information given by the `classtype` attribute, adding context.

These are some example of categorization:
* `ET TROJAN ABUSE.CH SSL Blacklist Malicious SSL certificate detected (Gozi MITM)`  should be categorized just as **TROJAN** because this is the main information about the rule
* `ET TROJAN WORM_VOBFUS Requesting exe` will also be categorized as **TROJAN**, but should not be categorized as **WORM_VOBFUS** because it does not make sense to have a category just for one malware
* `GPL TROJAN BackOrifice access` will also be categorized as **TROJAN**, not **GPL TROJAN**
* `GPL SMTP OUTBOUND bad file attachment` will be categorized as **SMTP**, the _OUTBOUND_ is not a categorization information
* `GPL SMTP SMTP relaying denied` will be categorized as **SMTP**, this is an example also shows the pattern used.

Rules coming from ET follow this convention on the `msg` field:
```
<SET> <CATEGORY> <desription>
```

However, looking into Talos' rules for SNORT, I noticed they use a different convention on `msg:
```
<CATEGORY>-<SUBCATEGORY> <description>
```


with this in mind I think that a better solution whould be to add some code to the parser that can recognize if the `msg` field is following one of the above mentioned conventions and then parsing the field according to this (so also the issue #42 will be included in the feature). 

It whould be great if we can also make it work with other naming convention, but this will require more reasoning.

I made a comment:

> still I see some instability in their output even when interacting with them on the command line

However, experimenting some more, I see that the command-line results from these engines are stable, so long as I enter `ucinewgame` and re-submit the position every time before asking for an evaluation. Alternatively, if I quit the engine and always submit the request with a new instance. Otherwise, the engines cache some of their previous work, so you can't expect:

```
position blahblah
go yada yada
go yada yada
```

to deliver the same results for the two invocations of `go yada yada`, even if the `go` request specified a max depth to query.

However, none of this explains the varying responses I was getting through the python-chess interface, as shown above, because in that case, each query was submitted to a newly-spawned instance of the engine.

And there's another issue, from dst=True to dst=False, there is a repeated hour, which is not handled correctly:
```
from apscheduler.triggers.cron import CronTrigger
from datetime import datetime, timedelta
import pytz
utc = pytz.utc
cet = pytz.timezone('CET')
t = CronTrigger(hour='*', minute=30, timezone=cet)
now = utc.localize(datetime(2015, 10, 24, 22, 15))
last_f = cet.normalize(cet.localize(datetime(2015, 10, 25, 1, 30)))
for _ in range(4):
    print 'last_f %s (utc %s), now %s' % (last_f, last_f.astimezone(utc), now)
    last_f = t.get_next_fire_time(last_f, now)
    now += timedelta(hours=1)
```
gives the following output -- note that an hour is skipped:
```
last_f 2015-10-25 01:30:00+02:00 (utc 2015-10-24 23:30:00+00:00), now 2015-10-24 22:15:00+00:00
last_f 2015-10-25 02:30:00+01:00 (utc 2015-10-25 01:30:00+00:00), now 2015-10-25 01:45:00+00:00
last_f 2015-10-25 03:30:00+01:00 (utc 2015-10-25 02:30:00+00:00), now 2015-10-25 02:45:00+00:00
last_f 2015-10-25 04:30:00+01:00 (utc 2015-10-25 03:30:00+00:00), now 2015-10-25 03:45:00+00:00
```
I would have expected 2:30 to be hit twice and the hour in the utc/now columns should not leave out 00:30/23:45:
```
last_f 2015-10-25 01:30:00+02:00 (utc 2015-10-24 23:30:00+00:00), now 2015-10-24 22:15:00+00:00
last_f 2015-10-25 02:30:00+02:00 (utc 2015-10-25 00:30:00+00:00), now 2015-10-25 23:45:00+00:00  # missing
last_f 2015-10-25 02:30:00+01:00 (utc 2015-10-25 01:30:00+00:00), now 2015-10-25 01:45:00+00:00
last_f 2015-10-25 03:30:00+01:00 (utc 2015-10-25 02:30:00+00:00), now 2015-10-25 02:45:00+00:00
last_f 2015-10-25 04:30:00+01:00 (utc 2015-10-25 03:30:00+00:00), now 2015-10-25 03:45:00+00:00
```

The problem appears to stem from ```_increment_field_value``` where the hour value "2" is incremented to "3". This crosses the dst threshold.

Usually, this is coped with by internally working in UTC and UTC only. But with apscheduler CronTrigger's powerful cron-like syntax (e.g. hour="1,5,6,21"), that approach appears quite cumbersome.
In the above example, one might have to increment the hour of the utc datetime "0" to "1" and then translate it to cet.

So, unfortunately, I do not have any elegant and short solution at hand.

But maybe one comes up in the months until the next change from dst=True to dst=False (I guess that's roughly six months in the northern hemisphere, 12 months in the southern hemisphere).

Yes, you are correct. Garbage collection is basically unspecified, so in (for example) jython, Pypy or a future change in cpython that could happen. Technically, the open files aren't "garbage collected" but immediately closed as soon as there are no more references to it (in the cpython implementation!). Since there are never any references to it in the first place, they are closed as soon as the read is done in this case. More information here: http://stackoverflow.com/questions/575278/how-does-python-close-files-that-have-been-gced

> thousands of file descriptors are open if your inventory is big enough, right?

The code you modified relates to the reading of the `hosts` file, not the actual output of the `setup` module (the JSON files in the out/ dir). I'd be surprised if anybody has more than a few dozen `hosts` files.

So while it's *highly* unlikely that we would run into a problem with file descriptors in practice, using the `with` statement is the best practice to follow, as it is guaranteed to properly close the file descriptors in all Python versions!

Hey Sebastian,

For `make release`, the repo has to be clear of nearly everything (.pyc files, caching, generated example output, temp files, etc). A .gitignore won't help, since the package building process doesn't reference it. But if the `clean` rule is fully automatic, it might delete unstaged changes, which is the last thing I'd want. An automated build / release wouldn't have those files anyway (cause it wouldn't have been touched by humans), so the `git clean -i` won't raise any questions anyway.

Initially I had separate commands in the `clean` section to remove .pyc files, etc, but it took a bit too much maintenance and regularly screwed up test-released I'd make.

Perhaps we could do the following:

1. `make clean` automatically cleans only files generated by other Make rules.
2. `make release` refuses to work unless the repo is clean. This would remove the interactive aspect of the rule, but still prevent accidental deletion of unstaged changed.

In your blueprint module, add the following import or merge it with your current one:

You can make a helper function that will initialize and store the oauth client on the instance of the Flask app doing something like:

```python
def get_oauth_client(app):
    if hasattr(app, 'oauth_client'):
        return app.oauth_client
    else:
        client = OAuth(app).remote_app(
            'twitter',
            consumer_key=os.environ['TWITTER_CONSUMER_KEY'],
            consumer_secret=os.environ['TWITTER_CONSUMER_SECRET'],
            base_url='https://api.twitter.com/1.1/',
            request_token_url='https://api.twitter.com/oauth/request_token',
            access_token_url='https://api.twitter.com/oauth/access_token',
            authorize_url='https://api.twitter.com/oauth/authorize')
        app.oauth_client = client
        return client
    return None
```
_(Note: I'm assuming you're only using 1 OAuth client, but you can adapt the above to a list or dictionary of clients.)_

When you need the client, like in an authorization or callback function, reference it using the helper function. Example:

```python
@oauth_api.route('/callback')
def oauth_callback():
    resp = get_oauth_client(current_app).authorized_response()
    token = str(resp['oauth_token']) + ',' + str(resp['oauth_token_secret'])
    # do something with the token
```

I really have no idea how to implement that. If you see the readchar_linux.py code, everything reduces to read from stdin (just `sys.stdin.read(1)`). The problem with ESC key is the code it generates: 0x21, just the first code generated by F1-F12 or any ALT/CTRL combination.

How can it be implemented? Well... I find three ways:
- by adding a parameter to return the ESC code when read. If it is set to true, the ESC management is delegated to the caller. I really hate this solution.
- by checking if there is any character in stdin, ready to be read. This is the best one, but there is no primitive to do that.
- by creating two threads. The first one will read the char and the other will give some time to get the char. If no additional character is ready by then, then return just the ESC key.

The last solution may fit the bill, but it can generate unexpected results: it could return different results depending on the time it requires to be generated. And it may add an important delay (just a few milliseconds but for each `readkey` call).

I really do not want to do that, so I think I will leave this issue as open, so anyone can suggest another solution or implementation.

The design of pyswagger to perform an Operation Object is to return a tuple of (SwaggerReqeust, SwaggerResponse). If you just use it in this form:
```python
client.request(op(user="Tom"))
```
The tuple of SwaggerRequest, SwaggerResponse is silently pass to client.request. If you need more customization, you need to pass them in this form
```python
req, resp = op(user="Tom")
req.mime .... # any customization
resp.xxxx

client.request((req, resp,)) # pass them as a tuple
```

If you have 10 minutes of someone speaking, there will be pauses in the audio that are very low in volume. The dynamic range is the difference between the maximum volume and the minimum volume, hence, there will be quite some dynamic range in your file.

Peak normalization (e.g., normalizing to a peak value of 0 dBFS in the following example) only lifts the gain, but the dynamic range of the file will be the same:

![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/582444/30521800-a3f7f3a8-9bc5-11e7-8b50-569d51b71352.png)

If you want to minimize the dynamic range you need a heavy compressor or some other form of dynamic normalization where only *parts* (windows) of that waveform are normalized. Note that if you do that too extremely you'll just amplify the noise floor of the recording, meaning that you'll get a lot of random noise in speaker pauses.

`valid_score` is an ad-hoc correctness check. See the docs of the MRZ class:

```
        The parsing computes three validation indicators:
            valid_check_digits - a list of booleans indicating which of the "check digits" in the MRZ were valid.
                                TD1/TD2 has four check digits, TD3 - five, MRVA/B - three.
                                The separate booleans are also available as valid_number, valid_date_of_birth, valid_expiration_date, valid_composite
                                and valid_personal_number (TD3 only).
            valid_line_lengths - a list of booleans, indicating which of the lines (3 in TD1, 2 in TD2/TD3) had the expected length.
            valid_misc         - a list of booleans, indicating various additional validity checks (unspecified, see code).
        The valid_score field counts the "validity score" according to the flags above and is an int between 0 and 100.
```

Here's the actual computation:
```
        self.valid_score = 10*sum(self.valid_check_digits) + sum(self.valid_line_lengths) + sum(self.valid_misc) + 1
        self.valid_score = 100*self.valid_score//(40+3+1+1)
```


In your case you can see that the checksums for the date of birth and expiration date are wrong (in particular, expiration date is not even a date). The personal code is lacking along with the corresponding check digit, hence these are also deemed wrong. The composite check digit is also lacking and hence wrong.

It may be the case that the particular document you are reading is simply not following the standards of how the check digits should be computed.

@IntegersOfK @CamDavidsonPilon Digging into the code, I think I found the issue with why the serialization via pickle fails: 
when we instantiate a t-digest structure, we create an accumulation tree and the latter requires a lambda for aggregration at the nodes. If the lambda is also passed to the t-digest structure, that can resolves the issue. 

To reproduce it:

```python
# import pickle
import pickle
from tdigest import TDigest
from accumulation_tree import AccumulationTree
from numpy.random import random

digest = TDigest()

def acc(x):
    return x.count
digest.C = AccumulationTree(acc)

for x in range(5000):
    digest.update(random())

f = open('test.td', 'wb+')
pickle.dump(digest, f, protocol=pickle.HIGHEST_PROTOCOL)
```

which is what we expect: we created a file with random data of size 10MB (file `large.1`) and encrypted that to create `large.2`, then **de**crypted `large.2` to get `large.3` (the `example-cmd.py` automatically detects encrypted files and decrypts them).  as you can see, `large.1` and `large.3` are identical, but `large.2` is different, because it is encrypted.

Thanks for the info. I was able to reproduce the bug.

Unfortunately, this is actually a Numpy limitation that also uncovers a bug. I am going to open two issues with Numpy on this. There is a work around.

# Numpy Stuff (skip if you just want the work around)

Running ``hdf5storage.loadmat`` is basically doing a ``hdf5storage.read(path='/')`` which is reading the root of the file as if it was a structured ndarray. The two variables are read successfully and their dtypes obtained. The error happens when the dtype for the structured ndarray to hold them both together is constructed. I put in a print statement for ``dt_whole`` and got the following.

``[('a', dtype('float64'), (8000000, 80)), ('b', dtype('float64'), (8000000, 176))]``

Running that through ``np.dtype`` spits out the value error. The structured ndarray to hold this data is one element, but that one element has two huge fields. The size of the element is just larger than what can be handled. If I run it on a smaller one such as

``` python
c = np.dtype([('a', 'float64', (3300000, 80)), ('b', 'float64', (800000, 176))])
c.itemsize
````
I get the value ``-1056567296``, which makes is obviously wrong. If we calculate it by hand, the size should be ``3238400000``. If we try to store that as a 32-bit signed integer, which is ``- (2**31) + (3238400000 - 2**31)``, we get ``-1056567296``.

So Numpy is using 32-bit signed integers for dtypes. This can be seen in ``np.dtype([('a', 'int8', 2**31-1)])`` working but ``np.dtype([('a', 'int8', 2**31)])`` producing the same error. I don't exactly know how it didn't produce an error with the previous example. My guess is that it is not doing overflow checking.


# Work Around

If you read the two variables individually, this whole problem is circumvented. If their names in matlab were ``'a'`` and ``'b'``, then you would run

``` python
hdf5storage.loadmat('file.mat', variable_names=['a', 'b'])
```
which will read the specified names individually.


# Long Term Fix

In addition to hoping that Numpy fixes some of these issues in their next version, I am going to change the ``hdf5storage.loadmat`` command to, when not told which variables to load, just read ``'/'`` and extract the contents, it will instead get the list of all HDF5 Datasets and Groups under ``'/'`` and read them individually. This will also have the advantage of saving memory and processing time used in doing the extraction (memory copy).

I found a particular solution for this kind of issue:

- set event in summer (DST), but have recurrent occurrences in winter (no DST)
- set event in winter (no DST), but have recurrent occurrences in summer (DST)

I use [DRF](http://www.django-rest-framework.org/) so I have a serializer for Occurrence model.

I just apply an offset to the occurrence start/end, depending on the event start/end DST offsets, so that there won't be any issues with recurring occurrences throughout the year.

Do you think this would be useful to add in django-schedule core, when computing the recurrent occurrences? Assuming we store the UTC datetime in database.

```
class OccurrenceSerializer(serializers.HyperlinkedModelSerializer):
    start = serializers.SerializerMethodField(method_name='get_start_dst_adjusted')
    end = serializers.SerializerMethodField(method_name='get_end_dst_adjusted')

    class Meta:
        model = schedule_models.Occurrence
        fields = ('id', 'title', 'description', 'start', 'end')

    def get_start_dst_adjusted(self, obj):
        event_start_dst_offset = timezone.localtime(obj.event.start).dst()
        occurrence_start_dst_offset = timezone.localtime(obj.start).dst()

        offset = event_start_dst_offset - occurrence_start_dst_offset

        return obj.start + offset

    def get_end_dst_adjusted(self, obj):
        event_end_dst_offset = timezone.localtime(obj.event.end).dst()
        occurrence_end_dst_offset = timezone.localtime(obj.end).dst()

        offset = event_end_dst_offset - occurrence_end_dst_offset

        return obj.end + offset
```

I'd upvote this for implementation. Can't imagine it's too hard, though may need to support a slightly richer URL syntax. Currently:

http://site.tld/model?field1=val1&field2_lt=val&field3_gt=val3

is all AND logic. To support OR logic we could introduce a new syntax. Just floating an idea it could be as simple as introducing some new URL parameters, like **and** and **or** 

http://site.tld/model?field1=val1&and&field2_lt=val2&or&field3_gt=val3

That's possible because order exists on the URL and I'd moot that such a string would be parsed right to  producing:

`Q(field1=val1)  & (Q(field2_lt=val2) | Q(field3_gt=val3) )`

Of course for true generality we'd need to be able to group items on the URL, but by same reasoning, two parameters, lb and rb perhaps can encode braces. 

Alternately the whole thing can be encoded in one parameter "filter" with one of the very few URL friendly chars as a delimiter:

http://site.tld/model?filter=lb~field1~val1~and~field2_lt~val2~rb~or~field3_gt=val3

might parse to:

`(Q(field1=val1)  & Q(field2_lt=val2)) | Q(field3_gt=val3) `

Anyhow, I think supporting OR logic on the URL would be a major value add to django-url-filter.

Just my thoughts anyhow.

It is technically possible to expand a to-many relation while listing a set of resources. (just make sure you add the expandable field to `permit_list_expands` in your viewset). However, I've moved away from this practice. If your API has multiple consumers, it is hard to satisfy all use cases. Some may want to filter the expanded groups, or not all callers should be allowed to view the expanded resources. So, you have a more stable, maintainable API if you ask the consumers to make more HTTP requests. You could give them an endpoint that allows them to filter groups by users, for instance: `/groups?users=id1,id2,id3,id4`.

Mailchimp does not accept obvious / generic emails such as me@example.com or john.doe@example.com

Closing this issue

Our API does not process queryparams arguments at all, as that is expected to be processed be the end user beforehand. We perform processing on any actual post data sent through as those will cause hard errors, but logic errors caused by incorrect queryparams do not technically cause the API to malfunction, it simply returns unexpected results. My plan is to simply update the docstring wherever those queryparams are indicated (which I believe is more endpoints that simply campaigns.all()). If there is dissenting opinion from the other maintainers, we will look into a different solution as needed.

I will need to update the documentation for that section. The bit about count defaulting to 10 and offset defaulting to 0 is talking about the values if nothing is provided. The documentation is missing mention that get_all forces those values to change. We fetch 5000 records at a time to limit the number of calls to the service and hopefully prevent calls from hitting any limits that MailChimp places on accounts. It might be a useful enhancement to allow an option to configure the value used by the `_iterate` function to something other than 5000, but when a list can have 100,000 emails, performing that in 10,000 calls would be massive overkill.

Hi David,

A couple of things. Firstly, is your intention to be giving the form GET or POST data for validation? Or to give it initial values? From your code snippet above it looks like you're giving GET or POST data, but you say your providing initial values. If you are intending on giving initial values, you should specify them like:

```
form(initial=data)
```

Secondly, is there any reason to give the raw address value as opposed to the address object itself? The `AddressWidget` prefers to receive address objects, as it can then properly determine the various components of an address. If the a raw value is given those components will not be returned, even if they are previously available.

Cheers,
Luke

Hello!

Well, hvad does not have the concept of a default language. All translations are equal and treated as such. You can specify a fallbacks chain when querying for a language, but it applies to the translation as a whole, not field-by-field. Thus, if your object has a translation in some language, then all fields will use that translation.

What you want to do is completely possible, but you'll have to implement the logic by yourself. Basically, that means at some point, either in your views or client-side, you must load both the language you want *and* the language you want to use as a default, and use your own logic to display either depending on some condition or, why not, both side by side.

If your REST api is intended for displaying only, doing this server-side to hide the implementation detail of how the video is chosen makes sense. On the other hand, if your REST api is read-write to allow editing the fields, it should simply expose the fact that the video is `null` and let your javascript load another language if it so wishes.

Okay, found a bunch more problems:

- You are using gevent, which does not support websocket natively. The gevent-websocket package does not run on Python3, so to I switched to eventlet, which has native support for websocket.
- You did not monkey patch the standard library for eventlet/gevent, which is required when using third party libraries that do sockets/threads/etc. I added this at the top of your `app.py` script.
- The `app.py` uses Flask-SocketIO, but the `bot.py` script uses python-socketio. These two have different defaults regarding the redis channel they use to communicate. Add `channel='flask-socketio'` to the constructor on `bot.py` to make the two use the same channel.

FWIW, I would probably implement it so that as long as you wrap the factory function in a special type so it can be unique identified, that I overload the passed in wrapped argument.

There is a similar construct used in the decorator when supplying an adapter definition to change the type specification.

```
class AdapterFactory(object):
    def __call__(self, wrapped):
        raise NotImplementedError()

class DelegatedAdapterFactory(AdapterFactory):
    def __init__(self, factory):
        super(DelegatedAdapterFactory, self).__init__()
        self.factory = factory
    def __call__(self, wrapped):
        return self.factory(wrapped)

adapter_factory = DelegatedAdapterFactory
```

Which is used as:

```
    def test_adapter_factory(self):
        def factory(wrapped):
            argspec = inspect.getargspec(wrapped)
            argspec.args.insert(0, 'arg0')
            return argspec

        @wrapt.decorator(adapter=wrapt.adapter_factory(factory))
        def _wrapper_1(wrapped, instance, args, kwargs):
            return wrapped(*args, **kwargs)
```

The actual code then has a check:

```
            if adapter:

```

In the case we are talking about would use it as:

```
import wrapt

def expensive_func():
    from time import sleep
    print('starting calculation')
    # just as example for a very slow computation
    sleep(2)
    print('finished calculation')
    # return the result of the calculation
    return 10

obj = wrapt.Proxy(wrapt.wrapped_factory(expensive_func))
# function is called only when object is actually used
print(obj)  # now expensive_func is called

print(obj)  # the result without calling the expensive_func
```

IOW, based on see special factory type, change what happens internally such that flagged that need to do lazy setting of wrapped object first time it is required.

So you don't need a special type of the lazy case, would be part of the one proxy class implementation.

Here's the idea.

In refs.bib:

```
@article{wald1939, ...}
```

In doc1.rst:

```
:cite:`local1-wald1939`

... bibliography: refs.bib
    :tag: local1
    :prefix: A
```

In doc2.rst, or really anywhere (could even be again in doc1.rst!!):

```
:cite:`local2-wald1939`

... bibliography: refs.bib
    :tag: local2
    :prefix: B
```

The prefix makes sure that no duplicate labels occur (this is only necessary if you know that you will be referring to the same document in multiple places). There's no way around that: sphinx always treats citations as global no matter what.

Does this seem sensible from a user perspective?

is not sufficient. context needs to be a reference to an existing instance in session. But cls is a class and not an instance and is not tied to session at all. To find the  instance in session that corresponds to obj -if it exists- you need data from obj to identify this instance. To do this, i extract the primary key from cls and use this to extract the identity of obj in session. (This works only if a primary key exists).
```
session.query(cls).get(identity)
```
then returns this instance or None. None means, a new instance is created and added to session.

This solution works for me for a single column primary key. I did not test it for composite primary keys, but (I hope) the only difference is, the key is a tuple in this case.

I updated the ```update_mapfield``` function to fix it.

```
def update_mapfield(instance, validated_data, field, embedded_doc):
    """
    updates mongoengine MapField by iterarting over keys
    :param instance: instance to be modified (document)
    :param validated_data: dict with new data
    :param field: field to be updated
    :param embedded_doc: embedded document class
    :return: None
    """
    for key, value in validated_data[field].items():
        for k, v in value.items():
            if k in value:   # for making sure that the already set field doesn't get overwritten with null
                try:
                    if isinstance(instance[field][key][k], list):
                        # update_set updates the list field instead of overwriting it
                        instance[field][key][k] = update_set(instance[field][key][k], validated_data[field][key][k])
                    else:
                        instance[field][key][k] = validated_data[field][key][k]
                   instance[field][key][k] = embedded_doc(validated_data[field][key][k])
                except KeyError:
                    instance[field].update({key: embedded_doc(**value)})
    instance.save()
```

@zhenyisx yeah, it retries forever on that node due to this loop:
https://github.com/yahoo/TensorFlowOnSpark/blob/master/tensorflowonspark/TFNode.py#L52-L78

Note that we are somewhat following the notion of YARN resource allocation (i.e. wait until the resources become available)... and also that we are restricted to this machine because of the TF cluster_spec reservation scheme.

Theoretically, you can remove this loop along with the `try/except`, s.t. the exception is surfaced to Spark.  This, in turn, would signal a task failure, which would eventually end up with a job failure.

@nextghost My considerations:

1) Let's not mix atomic updates with non-atomic ones. `db(somequery).update(values)` and `record.update_record(values)` are totally different operations; for instance if you have to update a bank account you should write 

```python
db(db.accounts.id == someid).update(amount=db.accounts.amount - transaction_value)
```

because this

```python
account = db.accounts(someid)
account.update_record(amount=account.amount-transaction_value)
```
won't protect you in any case, even with a record lock, from updating a value which is not correct. 

2) I understand the problem on `update_or_insert` but I think locking is not the answer. The risk is to introduce deadlocks and it's quite hard to guess the side effects. Moreover, every database use its own way to handle this, for example Postgres 9.5 introduced the UPSERT condition. Before that you could use IF conditions in postgres, but in this case everyone suggest to lock the entire tables to avoid race conditions, which feels like not a solution under my pov.

3) I agree to find a solution for this, but I think the best option is to rewrite the `update_or_insert` method differently, to try making it more *atomic*, and be honest with developers about the consistency of that operation. I'm for the thing "if you want to lock something, you write it on your own because you know what you're doing".

The behaviour we've implemented (which I said was "_not_ expected behaviour" above) is the "usual" behaviour according to [this Stack Overflow answer](http://stackoverflow.com/a/15568275/1821). @szha  thank you very much for your reply. I am trying to implement the pyramid scene parsing pooling layer, from [here](https://hszhao.github.io/projects/pspnet/) . For this implementation one needs to perform max pooling (or other) in a set of scales: a) global pooling, b) 1/2, c) 1/4, d) 1/8, then rescale and then concatenate results. Full details in the [arxiv paper](https://arxiv.org/abs/1612.01105). My implementation for this module (am interested only in the pooling module, not the whole network  architecture), is the following (works with NDArray, but I cannot call hybridize): 


```Python

from mxnet import gluon
from mxnet.gluon import  HybridBlock
from mxnet.ndarray import NDArray
from conv2Dnormed import *

class PSP_Pooling(HybridBlock):

    """
    Pyramid Scene Parsing pooling layer, as defined in Zhao et al. 2017 (https://arxiv.org/abs/1612.01105)        
    This is only the pyramid pooling module. 
    INPUT:
        layer of size Nbatch, Nchannel, H, W
    OUTPUT:
        layer of size Nbatch,  Nchannel, H, W. 

    """

    def __init__(self, _nfilters, _norm_type = 'BatchNorm', **kwards):
        HybridBlock.__init__(self,**kwards)

        self.nfilters = _nfilters

        with self.name_scope():

            self.conv1 = gluon.nn.Conv2D(self.nfilters/4,kernel_size=(1,1),padding=0, prefix="_conv1_")
            self.conv2 = gluon.nn.Conv2D(self.nfilters/4,kernel_size=(1,1),padding=0, prefix="_conv2_")
            self.conv3 = gluon.nn.Conv2D(self.nfilters/4,kernel_size=(1,1),padding=0, prefix="_conv3_")
            self.conv4 = gluon.nn.Conv2D(self.nfilters/4,kernel_size=(1,1),padding=0, prefix="_conv4_")

        # Hopefully this creates a list of functions by reference 
        self.convs = [ self.conv1, self.conv2, self.conv3, self.conv4 ]

        # This is a custom Conv2D followed by either BatchNormalization or InstanceNormalization layer
        self.conv_norm_final = Conv2DNormed(channels = self.nfilters,
                                            kernel_size=(1,1),
                                            padding=(0,0),
                                            _norm_type=_norm_type)



    def hybrid_forward(self,F,_input):

        # This works ONLY for NDArray input :( 

        # Also this if statement could be slowing down the performance. 
        if isinstance(_input,NDArray):
            layer_size = _input.shape[2]
        else :
             raise NotImplementedError
#             layer_size = _input.infer_shape()
#             print layer_size


        p = [_input]

        for i in range(4):
            # @@@@@@@@@@@@@ Important part @@@@@@@@@@@@@@@
            # This is where I need to know the layer size, so I can apply appropriate pooling
            pool_size = layer_size / (2**i)
            # @@@@@@@@@@@@@ @@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@
            x = F.Pooling(_input,kernel=[pool_size,pool_size],stride=[pool_size,pool_size],pool_type='max')
            x = F.UpSampling(x,sample_type='nearest',scale=pool_size)
            x = self.convs[i](x)
            p += [x]

        out = F.concat(p[0],p[1],p[2],p[3],p[4],dim=1)

        out = self.conv_norm_final(out)
        out = F.relu(out)

        return out
```

There are two possible memory issues here with regards to autograd. (1) unnecessary forward data is saved, which should be fixed by https://github.com/apache/incubator-mxnet/commit/7d6385a515dbda096560271628abf34e91e9be63 ; (2) backward memory cannot reuse forward memory since autograd requires a standalone backward graph to be executed. I would try two things:
(1) Make sure that using pure symbols give the same memory consumption as tf+keras. For example, you can try use SymbolBlock.
(2) Do forward with autograd turned on, but without calling "compute_gradient". In this case, the forward data required by backward will be saved.

If both (1) and (2) have the same memory consumption compared with tf+keras, then the reason might due to forward and backward memory cannot be reused in autograd mode.

@eric-haibin-lin I agree the first benefit is important. I wonder if we can have forward() to pull required weights from kvstore automatically? to enable this, we'll need the operators that support sparse row weights to tell the executor which rows should be pulled. we probably need to add another attribute to NNVM for these operators.

For the second benefit, I would argue that if you accept maintaining the entire weight matrix in each worker node, you can create an array lookup table (an array that maps row id to the physical location in the row sparse weight matrix) instead of a hashtable to reduce the lookup overhead. Maintaining such an array lookup table requires computation of O(nzr), the number of non-zero rows, for both cleaning the existing table and inserting entries for a new row sparse weight matrix. Maintaining the entire weight matrix in each worker node has similar overhead for updating rows. The lookup table has memory of O(n), instead of O(n * p), the size of the entire weight matrix.

@jiarenyf Good question. As I understand, this function is written to return two things: the ctc loss (which is being optimised and used for back-propagation) and the softmax over the classes across time which can then be used for other purposes (but we don't want to optimize for this).

Since this `lstm_unroll` function returns a symbol, and ideally we'd want to call backwards on this resultant symbol, we need to make both components of the returned symbol losses (even if we don't really want to use the softmax part as a loss). Otherwise the symbol would expect to be passed a gradient when backward method is called (which we don't want).

Given we've made the both losses with `MakeLoss` we have to then 'disable' the gradient propagation for the softmax component, and this is done with `BlockGrad`. So to answer your question I think we need to keep these lines.

@johnnason I understand now. However I think your test might not be testing what you think it is :) When you call `ti.xcom_pull()` without an `execution_date` or `include_prior_dates=True`, it only pulls XComs that have the same execution_date as that TI. Since you never pushed an XCom for that execution_date, you should always return `None` regardless of the clear behavior. It's a good test to have, just not directly related to the bug you're fixing (I think). So to be honest I'd suggest making a second test `test_xcom_pull_different_execution_date` or something to make it clear that the last TI should *never* pull the previous TI's XCom without explicit instruction.

        session.query(Pool)
        .filter(Pool.pool == p)
        .first())
    if not pool:
        session.add(Pool(pool=p, slots=8))
        session.commit()


# -----------------------------------------------------------------\
# DEFINE THE DAG
# -----------------------------------------------------------------/

# Define the Dag Name. This must be unique.
dag_name = 'hanging_subdags_n16_sqe'

# Default args are passed to each task
default_args = {
    'owner': 'Airflow',
    'depends_on_past': False,
    'start_date': datetime(2016, 04, 10),
    'retries': 0,
    'retry_interval': timedelta(minutes=5),
    'email': ['your@email.com'],
    'email_on_failure': True,
    'email_on_retry': True,
    'wait_for_downstream': False,
}

# Create the dag object
dag = DAG(dag_name,
          default_args=default_args,
          schedule_interval='0 0 * * *'
          )

# -----------------------------------------------------------------\
# DEFINE THE TASKS
# -----------------------------------------------------------------/


def get_subdag(dag, sd_id, pool=None):
    subdag = DAG(
        dag_id='{parent_dag}.{sd_id}'.format(
            parent_dag=dag.dag_id,
            sd_id=sd_id),
        params=dag.params,
        default_args=dag.default_args,
        template_searchpath=dag.template_searchpath,
        user_defined_macros=dag.user_defined_macros,
    )

    t1 = BashOperator(
        task_id='{sd_id}_step_1'.format(
            sd_id=sd_id
        ),
        bash_command='echo "hello" && sleep 1',
        dag=subdag,
        pool=pool
    )

    t2 = BashOperator(
        task_id='{sd_id}_step_two'.format(
            sd_id=sd_id
        ),
        bash_command='echo "hello" && sleep 2',
        dag=subdag,
        pool=pool
    )

    t2.set_upstream(t1)

    sdo = SubDagOperator(
        task_id=sd_id,
        subdag=subdag,
        retries=0,
        retry_delay=timedelta(seconds=5),
        dag=dag,
        depends_on_past=False,
        executor=SequentialExecutor()
    )

    return sdo

start_task = DummyOperator(
    task_id='start',
    dag=dag
)

for n in range(1, 17):
    sd_i = get_subdag(dag=dag, sd_id='level_1_{n}'.format(n=n), pool='test_pool_1')
    sd_ii = get_subdag(dag=dag, sd_id='level_2_{n}'.format(n=n), pool='test_pool_2')
    sd_iii = get_subdag(dag=dag, sd_id='level_3_{n}'.format(n=n), pool='test_pool_3')

    sd_i.set_upstream(start_task)
    sd_ii.set_upstream(sd_i)
    sd_iii.set_upstream(sd_ii)

I think this will work for anyone who was able to do `pip install django_extensions`, which would install to `sys.prefix`. Also, as described in the original issue, [always installing the kernel as `django_extensions`](https://github.com/django-extensions/django-extensions/blob/9cea102ccfe376fdd5e6d1b933b46e4c1c4085a9/django_extensions/management/commands/shell_plus.py#L163) means you can really only have one kernel at a time per Python environment.

I think properly installing it to `user_kernel_dir` would require a unique kernel name, but that plus adding a setting or command line argument seems like extra work for something that will be confusing to people who aren't familiar with kernel specs. The value in installing to `user_kernel_dir` is that it's accessible to `jupyter` running in a different environment, but `shell_plus --notebook` runs the notebook in the same environment as `django`, so I think it makes sense to install the kernel in the same place.

All that said, I think it'd at least be worth adding a helpful error message if the kernel installation fails, and maybe even some additional info messages like `Installing Django Shell-Plus kernel to {sys.prefix}...`. But I'll also dig further into the kernel spec API for more ways to improve this. I don't think this really addresses #1104, which I understand to be a request for "shell_plus --lab".

Ok, perfect. And thanks! I am still doing some more testing with the help of @prncc. I will make a final commit when fully ready.

Two important points that @prncc raised that I didn't think about are:

1. this class, as is, will not support concurrent execution of different instances of the same task; this is because in order to pass parameters from outside of the notebook to the notebook a JSON file is created at a given location and multiple instances could potentially try to write to the same file at the same time.
The original use case of this class that I had in mind, however, is for simple pipelines of notebooks where concurrent execution is not a requirement. If concurrent execution is a requirement for a project, perhaps one should translate the notebook to a script and make it into a task using some other class (e.g. luigi.contrib.external_program.ExternalProgramTask).

2. the path attributes from self.input() and self.output() can be passed to the notebook only if the targets associated with the task's self.input() and self.output() have a path attribute. I am changing the code to catch the AttributeError and pass None if self.input()[...].path or self.output[...].path does not exist.

```python
from luiti import TaskDay

class SomeTask(TaskDay):

    def requires(self):
        if self.date_str > "2015-08-01":  # Having a starting day is very important!

        return result
```

`SomeTask` is depended on previous day task of itself, but it has a starting day that is "2015-08-01", so there's no dead loop or stack overflow in the `requires` function.

Kind of. Depending on the current load at some computing and storage facilities, the completeness checks can be slow. But usually, the number of requests is the bottle neck.

A typical use case for this feature is a tree of M tasks which all have the same N dependencies. When the completeness check of those N deps is load-intensive (say 1k network requests per dep), this N x 1k requests are executed once for tree building and repeated M times which we like to skip by disabling the check. Of course, we have to make sure that the targets of the deps don't change during the processing of the tree.

from luigi.contrib.jupyter_notebook_task import load_parameters

I solved it. This is an xcode bug.
To prevent xCode from compiling your models, you need to tell xCode to load the file into the app Resources as a Data file. You can do this by selecting the file and open the Utilities sidebar in xCode, then under the File Inspector, you will find the Identity and Type section (the first one). The Type selector will by default be set to Digital Asset Exchange (DAE), you just need it to change this option to Data. With this, xCode will not compile the collada xml file and the model will load correctly.
