Yeah in general if you know the region of the bucket, you set that region when you instantiate the client to ensure you do not hit redirect errors and minimize the amount of calls being made. Boto3 does a best effort in trying to redirect you to the correct location, but it may not happen due to whatever region the bucket is located or the name of the bucket.

Assuming you are not using signature version 4 for your signing method, one thing that you can try is setting your ``signature_version`` to ``s3v4`` when you create your [client config](https://github.com/boto/botocore/blob/develop/botocore/config.py#L20). It has a lot of logic related to redirecting sigv4 signed requests that should help you out. The one caveat is that if you set your region incorrectly, you may initially be making extra API request to locate the correct region of your bucket. So in the end, it is best to set the region correctly in the first place.

@augustz this appears to be a bug. I've come up with a workaround that you can use for the time being, and we will fix this in Botocore:

```python
import boto3
from io import BytesIO

b = s3.resource('s3').Bucket('my-bucket')
b.put_object(Key='test', BytesIO(b'\xe2'))
```

This tricks Botocore into thinking it is an open binary file, which it knows how to handle.

Objects are a sub-resource of Bucket, so to get `object_key` from `my_bucket` you would do something like this:

```python
import boto3

s3 = boto3.resource('s3')
bucket = s3.Bucket('my_bucket')
obj = bucket.Object('object_key')
```

Hope that helps!

If I understand it well, mmap-manager always keeps a region-list for some file, even it is empty (no regions remain), so client-counts are always 1.   You have to invoke `mman.collect()` to clear them.

On Windows, this is necessary before renaming/deleting git-repo, since if any (mmap) files open to some index/pack objects forbid these objects to move/delete.
This is explained in [`smmap.mman.force_map_handle_removal_win()`](https://github.com/gitpython-developers/smmap/blob/master/smmap/mman.py#L443-L469), but actually, invoking `mmap.collect()` SHOULD be enough, if client codes does not wast regions (leaks).

Nice! So that confirms that the problem is that MonkeyType is tracing function calls in the stdlib `typing` module, which it should not do. There should be a follow-up here for MonkeyType to be more robust when it runs into a TypeVar (or anything else without a `__qualname__`) as an argument. The more immediate question, though, is why is MonkeyType even trying to trace these calls. The default code filter (https://github.com/Instagram/MonkeyType/blob/master/monkeytype/config.py#L111) should prevent this, and it doesn't appear to me that you are installing any custom config that would override that.

My hunch is that the virtualenv-within-a-virtualenv scenario is confusing the default code filter and it is mistakenly failing to identify the location of the standard library. So fixing that would be the other follow-up here.

Probably the easiest workaround for you right now might be to set `MONKEYTYPE_TRACE_MODULES="pip"` environment variable, which should resolve the issue. It explicitly tells MonkeyType to only trace calls within the `pip` namespace and nowhere else.

FYI comparing `pip install -r requirements.txt` to the time it takes to *lock* a dependency graph is not going to be informative as a point of comparison.  Pip doesn't actually _have_ a resolver, not in any real sense.  I think I can describe the difference.  When pip installs your `requirements.txt`, it follows this basic process:

* Find the first requirement listed
  * Find all of its dependencies
  * Install them all
* Find the second requirement listed
  * Find all of its dependencies
  * Install them all
* Find the third requirement listed
  * Find all of its dependencies
  * Install them all

This turns out to be pretty quick because pip doesn't really care if the dependencies of _package 1_ conflicted with the dependencies of _package 3_, it just installed the ones in _package 3_ last so that's what you get.

Pipenv follows a different process -- we compute a resolution graph that attempts to satisfy _all_ of the dependencies you specify, before we build your environment. That means we have to start downloading, comparing, and often times even _building_ packages to determine what your environment should ultimately look like, all before we've even begun the actual process of installing it (there are a lot of blog posts on why this is the case in python so I won't go into it more here).

Each step of that resolution process is made more computationally expensive by requiring hashes, which is a best practice.  We hash incoming packages after we receive them, then we compare them to the hashes that PyPI told us we should expect, and we store those hashes in the lockfile so that in the future, people who want to build an identical environment can do so with the contractual guarantee that the packages they build from are the same ones you originally used.

Pip search is a poor benchmark for any of this, in fact any of pip's tooling is a poor benchmark for doing this work -- we use pip for each piece of it, but putting it together in concert and across many dependencies to form and manage environments and graphs is where the value of pipenv is added.

One point of clarification -- once you resolve the full dependency graph, installation order shouldn't matter anymore.   Under the hood we actually pass `--no-deps` to every installation anyway.

As a small side-note, pip search is currently the only piece of pip's tooling that relies on the now deprecated XMLRPC interface, which is uncacheable and very slow.  It will always be slower than any other operation.

A summary of the current situation:

* PATH-based discovery and pyenv discovery is unchanged.
* A `py.exe`-based discovery has been implemented on Windows. As long as `py.exe` is present in PATH, `--python=X.Y` will consult it to discover Python installations. This has some minor restrictions (not able to discover WOW6432 installations, for example).
* A standalone library, [pythonfinder](https://github.com/sarugaku/pythonfinder), has been developed aiming to replace the current strategy. The integration is in progress, but is far from being finished. (I feel maybe we should scale the integration effort back a little, and use it only for *discovery* for now).
* pyenv is still the only option available for auto-installation. I did a minor rewrite in #2572, but there’s no change in functionality.

I tried to use [pyflame](https://github.com/uber/pyflame) to get a better idea about what takes so much time when pipenv is checking for new packages. It's mainly the `pip` invocations which is taking time so I patched pipenv to run pyflame on each pip process with this patch against the current master branch (a2a19361):

```patch
diff --git a/pipenv/core.py b/pipenv/core.py
index 6fe230c8..732f92d8 100644
--- a/pipenv/core.py
+++ b/pipenv/core.py
@@ -1480,7 +1480,8 @@ def pip_install(
     quoted_pip = which_pip(allow_global=allow_global)
     quoted_pip = escape_grouped_arguments(quoted_pip)
     upgrade_strategy = '--upgrade --upgrade-strategy=only-if-needed' if selective_upgrade else ''
-    pip_command = '{0} install {4} {5} {6} {7} {3} {1} {2} --exists-action w'.format(
+    from uuid import uuid4
+    pip_command = 'pyflame --threads --output=/pipenv/flamegraph/{8}.txt --trace {0} install {4} {5} {6} {7} {3} {1} {2} --exists-action w'.format(
         quoted_pip,
         install_reqs,
         ' '.join(prepare_pip_source_args(sources)),
@@ -1489,6 +1490,7 @@ def pip_install(
         src,
         verbose_flag,
         upgrade_strategy,
+        uuid4(),
     )
     if verbose:
         click.echo('$ {0}'.format(pip_command), err=True)
```

I then concatenated the resulting files together to one and created a Flamegraph out of it. I did this both for a fresh install of the packages from the [test repository](https://github.com/Tenzer/pipenv-test), where all packages had to be installed, and then ran it again afterwards where no new packages were installed. The resulting Flamegraphs are here:
https://files.tenzer.dk/pipenv/fresh-install.svg
https://files.tenzer.dk/pipenv/no-new-packages.svg

The output from `time` is included in the sub-title of the graphs.

I am by no means an expert on pyflame output, but from what I can grasp by looking at `no-new-packages.svg`, it seems like a big proportion of the time spent by `pip` is spent on importing code that it needs in order to run. Potentially in the area of 60-70% of the time. This becomes clearer if you search for "importlib" in the top right corner, and then compare it to the `fresh-install.svg` graph.

Could we change pipenv to only spawn as many pip processes it needs, instead of one per package? I think the start up cost for pip makes it worth spending a bit more time up front on preparing a list of dependencies for pip, rather than only sending one to each process.

As far as I can see, the downside of not having a pip process per package, is that the progress bar becomes less useful as it will potentially jump from 0% to 100% due to one invocation of pip, but I see that as a small downside compared to the current resource usage.

If there's support for changing this, I wouldn't mind giving it a go. Any hints as to what to be aware of would be appreciated as well.

The upstream server tells you to contact a different LDAP server, so either the proxy should do that on its own (hiding the fact from the client) or it would pass that info on and the client might get references he cannot resolve (due to servers not reachable from his point in the network).

The implementation of the class should be rather simple (as it is just a list of LDAP URIs encoded in an ASN.1 BER Sequence):
    
    SearchResultReference ::= [APPLICATION 19] SEQUENCE
                                  SIZE (1..MAX) OF uri URI

       def __init__(self, uris):

If I'm not mistaken `required` is for whether or not the field must be present, as in the key is in the input data. That validation already worked as expected.
This prevents accepting an empty dictionary of translations and so it felt closer to `allow_blank` or [allow_empty](http://www.django-rest-framework.org/topics/3.2-announcement/#manytomany-fields-and-blanktrue).

Also, in the a use case of deleting translations, someone might still want the field to be `required` while accepting a request to delete all the translations with `'translations': {}`.

It might be clearer with examples:
```python
{
   # whichever fields, but no 'translations'
}
```

Accepted only if `required=False` even if `allow_empty=True`.
Use case could be a project with automatic translations where manual translations are preferred.
but still filled by the backend if not present.

```python
{
   'translations': {}
}
```

Accepted only if `allow_empty=True`, even if `required=True`.
Use case: deleting all existing translations.

Well, that's a thing about the new import system implementation as of 3.2 or 3.3 (I can never remember).  Previously there was no such "cache", but now it caches the directory listings of `sys.path` entries, and the cache is only invalidated if the modtimes for any of those directories change.  So if you have a new module appear on the path within a second of when the import system was last invoked you can run into trouble.  This is especially problematic on OSX.  I actually submitted [a patch](http://bugs.python.org/issue17330) for this a while back, but it was ultimately rejected since it just pushed the issue into even more obscure territory.  And while I'm still not happy with the situation I agree with the decision to reject the patch for now.

Hi @ofek, great work here! Thanks for putting it together. My apologies for the delayed response here.

So I'm inclined to agree with @ob-stripe above: I think this is absolutely useful and a good idea, but I'm not crazy about plumbing a new `requestor` through in a bunch of methods that already have some pretty messy overloaded arguments.

I did something similar to this recently [in stripe-ruby](https://github.com/stripe/stripe-ruby#configuring-a-client) whereby instead of mixing in more method parameters, we instead use a block to initiate a request under a specific client (i.e. "session" in the parlance of Requests):

``` ruby
conn = Faraday.new
client = Stripe::StripeClient.new(conn)
client.request do
  charge, resp = Stripe::Charge.retrieve(
    "ch_18atAXCdGbJFKhCuBAa4532Z",
  )
end
puts resp.request_id
```

It works by putting an active client to thread-local storage and then using that instead of the default client if it's available.

What do you think about trying something like this for stripe-python? It doesn't have the exact same block syntax that Ruby has, but I think a custom method that can be passed to a `with` block could be used to a similar effect. [Some information on how this might be accomplished here](http://preshing.com/20110920/the-python-with-statement-by-example/).

@emmanuelle thanks for raising the API question.  We should think deep thoughts about API more often.  I've been pondering this feature's API, in the context of a few choices made in the past which may not have been ideal.  Most of scikit-image is functional, and we've used this approach for drawing shapes.  However, it seems a class-based approach would be really powerful for generation - particularly iterative work.

Consider if we'd gone a different way:

``` python
novel_image = skimage.draw.ImageGenerator(shape)  # Placeholder name
novel_image.circle(center_coords, radius, label='Circle0')
novel_image.square(coords, label='Square0')
novel_image.show()  # via matplotlib, view in Jupyter

# Draw Circle0 'above' Square0
novel_image['Circle0'].level_raise()  
novel_image.show()

output_image = novel_image.generate()  # Final, full resolution image generated here
```

IMHO, this is very expressive and readable.

Contrast that to the current state; to do anything like the above requires painstaking full-array modification at every intermediate step, and if you want to change anything you have to redo everything.  Slower and less expressive.

``` python
# How it would work now
novel_image = np.zeros(shape)
novel_image[skimage.draw.circle(r, c, radius)] = 1
novel_image[skimage.draw.rectangle(start, end)] = 2
plt.imshow(novel_image)

# Cannot simply overwrite in nearly all real cases, must regenerate
novel_image = np.zeros(shape)
novel_image[skimage.draw.rectangle(start, end)] = 2
novel_image[skimage.draw.circle(r, c, radius)] = 1
plt.imshow(novel_image)
```

This isn't meant to block the PR, just to raise the question: which API would you prefer?  Happy to continue the discussion in another Issue, but it's at least close to on topic here.

A bool array should be converted to uint8 before saved to a format that only supports uint8, no?  We do the same for float images.

Only a couple filters work that way, where we re-expose functionality from the SciPy NDImage subpackage. Generally speaking the best path forward is to place the entire implementation into its own file in our package unless it's already available in `scipy.ndimage`.

Also, I feel `skimage.features` would be a better home for COSFIRE. It is described as a "filter" in the paper, but its goal is keypoint and feature detection rather than modifying an image with a kernel and returning the result. The distinction here is subtle, but it certainly feels more in line with ORB, BRIEF, and the other feature/keypoint extractors in `skimage.feature`.

Finally, the other feature/keypoint extractors like ORB and BRIEF expose a common API. It would be ideal if you used those as a base to build upon, both for your own convenience and for users.

The upgrades form a dependency graph (DAG). The order in which upgrades are applied is determined by a topological sort of the DAG which produces a partial order. Basically any upgrade without a dependent can be the ``latest`` upgrade, hence it's not possible to define any upgrade as the latest.

The create recipe script only makes a guess based on what you have in the applied column. You must always validate if the ``depends_on`` attribute is actually the right one that makes sense.

All in all, I think this is a feature and not a bug, so if ok with you I'll close it?

Two thoughts:

First, if the implementation is going to be written in terms of `id(key)`, then I really think that needs to be spelled out in the interface documentation. Otherwise, people are liable to write things like `txn.set_data(self.__class__.__name__ + '.mykey')`, which isn't guaranteed to have the same ID---once this API exists, it's likely to get used much more broadly then just in a ZODB Storage object. Even if they don't make that particular mistake, it seems like a good idea to spell out why the design is this way. (Plus, on PyPy and other implementations, primitives and such are 

Second and much more minor, on PyPy in certain cases, calling `id` a lot ["can lead to performance problem.](http://doc.pypy.org/en/latest/cpython_differences.html). (Also, some objects that have the same id under CPython as an implementation quirk are not guaranteed to by the language, and don't under PyPy, and vice-versa.) This is somewhat less a problem if this usage is well documented. But maybe we should allow for a `key` function that allows the user to determine how to hash the argument?

```python
   def data(self, ob, key=id):
      return self._data[key(ob)]
```

I believe this is because async_send_heap doesn't copy the heap you give it, it [holds a reference](https://spead2.readthedocs.io/en/latest/cpp-send.html?highlight=async_send_heap#_CPPv2N6spead24send6stream15async_send_heapERK4heap18completion_handler16s_item_pointer_t) to it until the transmission is complete. When you reach the end of each for loop iteration, the heap goes out of scope and is freed, ripping memory out from under the sending thread. When you sleep, you give enough time for the transmission to occur before the heap gets freed.

There are a few ways to deal with this:
- Allocate NHEAPS heaps up front and use one for each heap (obviously doesn't scale to an unbounded stream).
- Dynamically allocate each heap, then free it in the completion callback.
- Keep a pool of available heaps, and recycle them by updating the values they reference (again, returning heaps to the pool in the completion callback).

If one of those (for the purposes of this bug I'd suggest just doing the second) doesn't fix the crash then please re-open.

By the way, your code also has a race condition where you're updating the timestamp at the same time as the transmitter may be reading it. If you instead add it as
```c++
h.add_item(0x1600, timestamp);
```
it will be copied into the heap (and encoded in big endian for you); you'd need to change the descriptor to specify size `heap_address_bits`.

I usually use OSX (10.9.5 here) for compiling numexpr and I am not experiencing this.  I am closing this, but feel free to reopen if you continue to experience problems.

Unfortunately the NumExpr 2.6 reductions are single threaded so you will not see a performance improvement from using `sum` inside NumExpr.  I would suggest you try,

`dist_2 = ne.evaluate('(nodes - node) ** 2').sum(axis=1)`

I'm not clear on why for `float32` there's a difference between NumPy and NumExpr 2.6, but if it changes with doing the sumations as above with NumPy after the return then likely the problem is within the NE reduction machinery. Also try pre-allocation of your output to stop NumExpr from using output buffering,

`temp = np.empty_like( nodes )`
`ne.evaluate( '(nodes-node)**2', out=temp)`
`dist_2 = temp.sum(axis=1)`

That will be harder on your RAM and memory bandwidth, however.  Also try transposing your matrix and averaging over axis 0 instead, since I assume you have C-ordered arrays?  

How much RAM do you have?  Creating additional permanent arrays for nodes_sqr in memory is of course simple (for both NumPy and NumExpr) but your array is already 4 GB.  It may help to pre-compute this but on the other hand it may simply further restrict your memory bandwidth.  It would depend on a lot on your computer's configuration.

How many threads are you using for NumExpr?  Don't use hyperthreading with it, use the number of virtual cores.

I was having the same issue trying to run Spark 2.2, Sparkling Water 2.2.2, and sparklyr within R Studio. I tried it a bunch of different ways using different configurations suggested in this and previous threads. Having the right versions I believe helped: Spark 2.2.0, Sparkling Water 2.2.2 and h20 version 3.14.07
I got a suggestion in R Studio when connecting to Spark:

```
Detected H2O version 3.14.0.7. Please install H2O version 3.14.0.2, which is compliant with the latest Sparkling Water version for Spark 2.2.* -> Sparkling Water version 2.2.2

                       To update your h2o R package, copy/paste the following commands and then restart your R session:
                       
                       detach("package:rsparkling", unload = TRUE)
                       if ("package:h2o" %in% search()) { detach("package:h2o", unload = TRUE) }
                       if (isNamespaceLoaded("h2o")){ unloadNamespace("h2o") }
                       remove.packages("h2o")
                       install.packages("h2o", type = "source", repos = "https://h2o-release.s3.amazonaws.com/h2o/rel-weierstrass/2/R")

```
HOWEVER, I believe this may have contributed to the issue as I did this suggestion and it didn't help so I reverted back to version 3.14.0.7


Next, try to connect to Spark and run h2o_context() using as many stock settings as possible (i.e. get rid of all your conf and other settings). IF it works, then you know your configuration is somehow preventing h2o from booting (this is what was happening to me).

Finally I made changes to my configuration (followed the helpful guide here: https://stackoverflow.com/questions/37871194/how-to-tune-spark-executor-number-cores-and-executor-memory). I did notice in the Spark UI that I was never able to control the number of cores used (always at max) until I implemented the settings suggested in the guide. 

Here is what my config call looked like (48 core 256 GB RAM system):

```
config <- spark_config()
config$spark.executor.cores=5
config$spark.executor.memory="25g"
config$`sparklyr.shell.driver-memory` <- "6g"
config$`sparklyr.shell.executor-memory` <- "6g"
config$spark.executor.instances=9
config$spark.driver.memory="4g"
config$spark.dynamicAllocation.enabled='false'
```

For those of us using R and sparklyr be sure to set the sparklyr.shell.driver-memory and sparklyr.shell.executor-memory to above the default (1G) as h2o will not run properly at that memory setting.

Good Luck.

In this fast lookup scheme the shapes tell you for a given current pixel height where the boundary between staying in the pixel you are in and moving to the next pixel is.  So they need to be stored or accessible.

> individual pixel effects: ability to put in a flat field including effects down to the sub-pixel level. Cross-hatching pattern could then be simulated.

FYI, the current state of things on this item is that we can make a flat field that includes astrometric effects down to the ~pixel scale if the WCS is accurate to that level.  It takes a numerical derivative of the astrometric solution with +- 1 pixel for the discrete derivative.  Here is an example for DECam using Gary's astrometric solution that includes edge distortions, tree rings, etc:

https://github.com/gbernstein/pixmappy/pull/2#issuecomment-286642919

![image](https://cloud.githubusercontent.com/assets/623887/26362972/97c8b60e-3fad-11e7-9ad0-541525d01f21.png)

QE effects aren't there of course, but those would be very straightforward to add if you have a pixel-by-pixel map of QE variation to multiply the image.

Does it make sense for it to return `NoContent` if a schema is not defined? Or would you rather let it return `'No example response was defined.'`?

I believe it would make more sense to return `NoContent` since this is what [the spec](https://github.com/OAI/OpenAPI-Specification/blob/master/versions/2.0.md#response-object) recommends (emphasis mine):

> A definition of the response structure. It can be a primitive, an array or an object. **If this field does not exist, it means no content is returned as part of the response.** As an extension to the Schema Object, its root type value may also be "file". This SHOULD be accompanied by a relevant produces mime-type.

Also, I was considering using their petstore example as a reference, but it seems they don't abide by the spec themselves, specifically regarding this:

> It is not expected from the documentation to necessarily cover all possible HTTP response codes, since they may not be known in advance. However, **it is expected from the documentation to cover a successful operation response** and any known errors.

However, in some cases in [that file](http://petstore.swagger.io/v2/swagger.json), they only define error responses, so the mock server is returning error codes in those cases.

In light of this, I suggest:

  * returning a status code of `418` with `NoContent` when there is no "success" 2xx response defined; since this goes against the spec (unless there are legitimate use cases for API endpoints that always return error codes?)

  * in case the response is defined with no schema return the correct status code with `NoContent` since this is mentioned in the specification directly.

Does that make sense?

You are correct, there is no current support in pyral for adding or removing items from a collection.  The workaround is to specify the entire collection with all the items you want as the result.  To add an item to a collection you need to obtain the members of the collection (refs) and then add the ref to the additional item you want in the collection then update the target item holding the collection. To remove an item from a collection, you will need to obtain the collection members (refs) and then remove the ref of the item targeted for removal and then update the target item holding the collection.  If this doesn't address your problem, please provide a more specific scenario so that I can reproduce your behavior to see if your specific use case can be addressed or that a workaround is feasible.

Thanks for the advices, maybe indeed a better stratedgy than this PR is needed. About the two paths:

1. Reshape might be easily handled, but for `nobackprop `& `flip-gradient`, I think even more complexities will be included since they are manipulating the gradients, which means that maybe they should keep their own gradient memory. So, these nodes only share other's memory of forward values? Otherwise we may need to add special checking only to detect the existence of these two kinds of special nodes when calculating gradients.

In fact, for the first path, I think maybe the method in this PR is already the most convenient way to do this. Currently, the added complexity is only the checking for invalid inplace-operations and invalid usage of values which have been re-written. If all the conditions are covered in these checkings, there will be no way to fail silently. (And also for future enhancements, we should remember that memory sharing might exist). The biggest problem is the one about interface as discussed before, the inplace opration is assigned by the users.

2. This is an interesting path, and more powerful because maybe more optimizations could be included. But indeed there should be methods to tell the system that the nodes are not needed for further expansion and could be fused. One of the way is depending on the users to add some tags, but this is again violating the spirit as the current PR does. The other implicit way to automatically do this might need to rely on specific languages features like the construction/destruction of C++ objects, to ensure that the users cannot "hold" any to-be optimized nodes. But this still depends on the users' destructing them, and for python which deals with gc without the knowing of users this might be harder.

Indeed, the problem is that the system has no ideas of which part of the computation-graph could be safely optimized. Thus I think to perform these kinds of optimizations, the users should at least provide some information, maybe?

----------

Anyway, thanks for the discussions and indeed I have not been thinking from the perspective of users.
Therefore, should I close this PR? For read-only operations, I can only think of `Reshape` for full memory optimization, for `nobackprop `& `flip-gradient`, will introducing half-sharing (only forward values) be a good idea?

You have to take into account that the pretenders server is running as a separate service, and it is pre-programmed using a JSON-based protocol. As such, pretenders (the server) can be used in a language-agnostic way, and clients can be written in multiple languages (incidentally we are only providing a python client ATM). Pretenders is designed to be potentially hosted for usage from multiple clients.

So if you think of your feature request taking that into account, it would translate as:
- I want to have a way to upload some executable code to the pretenders server (let's say this would be limited to python code for the time being).
- The server would execute this code upon matching requests

This feature request then comes with various difficulties:
- How do we serialize executable code so that it can be run in the server? 
- What language do we use? Python? Or something designed to be more embeddable (e.g. lua)? Or do we design some sort of DSL?
- If python, would this be python code "as a string" or pickled in some form? Using python-native features like pickling would seriously limit our ability to make this feature usable by non-Python clients. If you pass this as a string, it loses the simplicity of this being a callable in your tests...
- How do we sandbox this executable code so that it cannot be a security hazard to our server environment? We cannot just `eval` this code on the server side, we'd need to parse it and limit what can be done from within it...

So as you see there are many questions to be answered and this is IMHO far from a trivial thing to do.

Or did you have something else in mind?

filesystem paths are byte strings encoded using sys.getfilesystemencoding() on python 2. On python 3 they are (unicode) strings (internally decoded using sys.getfilesystemencoding() on posix platforms, or passed as such from Windows “wide” console API).

That's because Khaled took removed the Python code that converts Type1 fonts when he adapted psautohint.py from autohint.py. It would be trivial to restore this. To answer Behdad's question more fully: the libpsautohint.so module works with bez file data, an early simplified version text version of  Type 1, one bez file data string per glyph. The Python wrapper uses fonttools to open an OpenType/CFF file, and convert each CFF T2 charstring in turn to to bez data, calls the libpsautohint module, gets the updated bez file data for the glyph, and writes that back to the OpenType file.   If the source font is 'CFF', the Python wrapper creates an minimal TTFont, adds the CFF file as a CFF table, then proceeds with case 1. If the source font is a Type 1 font, then the Python wrapper calls the  AFDKO tool 'tx' to convert it to CFF, then goes to case 2.

PM for Datastore here. Some quick insights.

1.      Our system spans many servers across multiple data centers (it's synchronously replicated across regions). To get the best performance, we have a series of caches for things like permission checking that will be your database until you send us non-trivial traffic to hit various servers and warm those caches. When doing benchmarking, longer tests will give more reliable indicates of actual production performance (more below).

2.      Yes, for individual entity reads we will be slower than Cloud SQL. We trade off some latency for much greater durability and availability guarantees, along with other properties like scale down to zero, etc. We will scale better and more consistently, so queries the the same results will have the same latency when you have 1GiB with 10 reads/second as when you have 1PiB and 10 million reads/sec.

3.      Yes, 50ms would be considered slow for a single entity read. I modified the above test (still not a great test) and ran it in Cloud Shell, getting numbers that would be closer aligned with expected performance:

Code:

from google.cloud import datastore

from time import time

ds = datastore.Client('project-id-goes-here')

        entity = ds.get(key)

    end = time() * 1000

    print("avg duration (n=100) : ", (end - start)/10)

Yes specifically it uses the page tokens as a progress indicator, not just as a means of breaking results into pages. This means you can get 0 results on first page, but then 10 results on the next page, so you really always want to check the page token. This is especially true for slower queries, which are most of them without an index e.g. logName=  .

currently the only way to build a "multi-platform" pex (e.g. with both osx and linux wheels) is via `pants`. the README conveniently leaves this bit out, but it is entirely possible to do this in pex itself - it just hasn't been implemented in the `pex` CLI yet.

there's an outstanding ticket for supporting both multi-platform and multi-interpreter pexes with some discussion here: https://github.com/pantsbuild/pex/issues/68

and there's a lingering PR to implement this out here: https://github.com/pantsbuild/pex/pull/201

which adds support for multiple rounds of resolution against both multi-interpreter and multi-platform. this is what is needed to make this work if anyone had time to pick this back up.

We had some discussion on Slack; we settled on:
1. When a `DigestFile` node is created, leasing its `Digest`.
2. Kicking off a background thread from Python which periodically acquires the fork lock, and calls into the Rust to acquire the Graph lock and traverse the Graph re-leasing any `DigestFile` nodes. The traversal will be very special-cased.
3. Kicking off a background thread from Python which periodically acquires the fork lock, and calls into the Rust to initiate a GC pass.

2 and 3 may end up being the same service (which can be modelled on the `FSEventService`), or may end up being separate.

We're going to punt on things which don't appear in the graph is `DigestFile` nodes for now (e.g. files in `Snapshots` received from remote execution).

Specifically IIUC if you use/distribute FreeImage then you're bound by either the GPL (v2 or v3), or the "FreeImage Public License" which is a rebranding of the Mozilla Public License v1.0. The situation is complicated, and it's *definitely* misleading to summarize it as being "BSD". (The GPL is, well, the GPL, and MPL 1.0 is a funky weak-copyleft license that's GPL-incompatible and deprecated -- Mozilla these days uses the substantially rewritten MPL 2.0.)

10K is an internal limit in Crate (if no `LIMIT` if specified in `SELECT` statement), not in crash.

However, don't use crash as a dump tool! The client does not stream data from the server, but loads the whole response into memory. Use `COPY TO` [docs](https://crate.io/docs/reference/sql/dml.html#import-export) instead. This will output `JSON` that can be imported easily into Crate again using `COPY FROM`.

While this introduces the functionality I wanted, @bcavagnolo expressed a preference for maintaining a File-like interface.

The idea we came up with was inspired by how the requests library does this, which is to:
1. Introduce a `StreamingFile` with `read`, `readlines`, and `iter_content` methods (some other file methods would not be implemented).
2. Add `Archive.fetch(url, stream=True)` which would return a `StreamingFile` instead of a `File`.

@ankurankan The individual discretize methods, can use other methods provided by the ```scipy.stats.rv_continuous``` class. Like, the ```RoundingDiscretizer``` uses the cdf of the distribution. There can be other methods that require nth order moments in their algorithm too. Note that the reason we use the fonttools subsetter ex-post, instead of subsetting the source UFOs (in ufo2ft, for example), is that it's trivial to remove glyphs from a font, its kerning or groups, but subsetting the feature file is not straightforward. Granted, we could remove all reference to a dropped glyph in all the feaLib AST statements, and drop the empty statements, blocks, etc. ... but calling the fonttools subsetter is too convenient.

That's a little bit different, because it's language modeling, and there's one output for every input.  Maybe you could argue that we should have used a `SequenceLabelField` for that one, instead of a `TextField`, but I'm not sure...  I think a `TextField` isn't a bad use for this - it's an arbitrary-length sequence of strings.

Yeah, I think I'm with Mark here - using a `TextField` for the decoder output seems pretty reasonable.

Looking at the fingerings on the bach piece shown on the README, I can see some discrepancies from what the orthodox fingering choice would be. For instance, you have 2,3,5 playing F, A, D. Having 5th finger stretch from third finger from A - D is a considerable distance. The typical choice there would probably be 1,2,5. Similarly, I see the left hand using doing 1,2,1 to play G,A,Bb. Here, using thumb to play the Bb is awkward because of the black key. Each of those would point to some tweaking with the weights you're assigning to each option. I'm also a bit unclear how many path options you're searching. 
And the following is only trying to be helpful. If you know all this, you can ignore. But when I was writing my algorithms for this before, my general approach was the following...

- Create a "database" of "costs of movement" for every possible finger movement combination for every possible key combo (up to about 1.5 octaves). That is, "from-finger" on "from-note" to "to-finger" on "to-note". For example,  C up to E going from thumb to middle finger. And then do that for every possible option. I wrote some functions to calculate what this would be. And those functions had to include special rules for when moving from a white key up to a black key, or black key up down to a white key. And even some sub-special rules for when you're using thumb on those black keys, cause the cost for those is significantly higher than other fingers. This database is pretty annoying, but it really just turns into a big hash and you only have to calculate it once.
- Once you have this database, you ingest the file, and you look at the notes, and in conjunction with your cost database, you have to find the lowest cost path algorithm. I implemented this using [Viterbi's Algorithm](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Viterbi_algorithm). I used it cause it actually allows a full search of all options in linear time, rather than exponential time.
- The lowest cost path is the fingering, so you just align it with the notes, and you're good to go.

I'm happy to talk more about this if you're interested. Great work getting started on this!

We have "extended" attributes for rich formats like CINE and NorPix. Analogously, filenames can be used to encode metadata such as timestamps. I think that "filepaths" or "filenames" should be in this category, and we can let the caller duck-type it.

In my own use of pims, there is one piece of code that extracts the timestamp metadata from the reader object and copies it to a separate data structure; the rest of my code uses "vanilla" pims attributes. I think this is a pattern that pims should support and encourage, since metadata tends to be messy.

On the application units I just found that and have added the above policy to it as well. 

Components and applications really suffer from the same problem when we are trying to determine if they are down. If the only docker state I can key off of is the die event then that can happen for any number of reasons. It also means that containers caught in a death loop of restarting will be constantly sending alerts.

For us to have a robust alerting system we need to only alert in the case of a container being stopped. This ensures that we have (unsuccessfully) tried to restart the container and now we need to alert support/operations.

I think the cases were a component finally starts because we fix some error case in the system seems like the wrong approach. Instead, if components died after trying to restart, when the error was fixed the user could just restart the components themselves.

> Which things should be running in their own threads?

Processes, not threads.

I think every *long* running thing which can be well isolated from the rest deserves it's own process.  We have to be conservative at first because there is overhead to IPC in a multithreading environment, both in terms of performance as well as complexity.  Categories I see us shooting for are.

- Database
- JSON-RPC (currently bundled with networking)
- Networking (likely subdividable)
- The Syncers (currently bundled with networking)

Summary of some off-github discussions:
#### Periodically (at least once per block) triggers the ShardTracker for new logs
1. By polling `ShardTracker.get_add_header_logs()`: https://github.com/ethereum/sharding/blob/efa573ed9effaaf0ef877a4e0aa8b046c8893b2c/sharding/handler/shard_tracker.py#L82
	* Some required modification in sharding repo:
		1. `from_block` issue: by default, it uses **the start of current period**, but it’s impossible to guarantee we won’t miss any single period in Trinity level. So we may need to set `from_block` by other means.
			* Possible solution: Tracking the main chain block number as the source of `from_block`.
		2. Parent chain reorg issue
			* Possible solution:
			    1. Tracking `blockhash`. Every round, querying the `blockhash` again and checking if it’s the same.
			    2. Detecting at web3.py layer.
2. Async
	1. Now the `ShardTracker` and `LogHandler`  are both non-async. So invoking those function may block the node.
	2. May also need async web3.py APIs: https://github.com/ethereum/web3.py/issues/657

#### Possible system architecture

The above `NotaryService` design is a part of `ShardSyncer`. Exploring the higher level of Trinity, it may be:
```
[trinity] ShardNode
	-> [p2p] ShardNode._p2p_server: ShardServer
  		-> [p2p] ShardServer.shard_syncer: ShardSyncer
```

Thanks! The first change looks harmless, so I've no problem there. For the second one, I'm concerned that it incompletely solves the problem (thanks for finding this bug, BTW). I'll describe the problem (as I understand it) below:

**Old behavior**: iterate over links and assign the label already assigned to either the start or end node or, if neither, a new label

**Problem**: transitive chains are not grouped when iteration starts at opposite ends of the chain (e.g. A->B then C->D then B->C) because two labels are created for each end before they are connected.

**New behavior**: same as old, but first sort links by the min of their start or end node

The proposed fix can fail in this situation, I think (sorted by lowest nodeid): 10004 --> 10007, 10005 --> 10006, 10006 --> 10007.

I think this is the "connected components" problem of graph theory. Here's how NetworkX does it: https://networkx.github.io/documentation/latest/_modules/networkx/algorithms/components/connected.html, and this answer on StackOverflow is essentially the same thing: http://stackoverflow.com/a/13837045/1441112. Basically, I don't think we can get around the need to group first and then assign labels.

Can you send the MRS or DMRS that caused the problem?

And for this PR, I can either accept-as is and then I'll fix it later, or you can fix the second problem and I'll merge the result.

Regarding upgrades of packages in general, I'd prefer conda to upgrade only under two circumstances:
* the update is explicitly requested by the user (by providing an explicit version spec, a command line option, or a global configuration flag), or
* the present installation request cannot be fulfilled otherwise.

In any case, conda should refuse (with suitable diagnostic output) to perform the upgrade if it would result in an inconsistent environment.

@fperez I just circled back.  Conda 4.3 was doing the right thing [here](https://gist.github.com/fperez/d9bc27bb0f1bd0faeb246888bc033263#file-conda-log-L380-L382)

    The package for six located at /Users/fperez/usr/conda/pkgs/six-1.10.0-py35_0
    appears to be corrupted. The path 'lib/python3.5/site-packages/six-1.10.0-py3.5.egg-info/SOURCES.txt'
    specified in the package manifest cannot be found.

If the package is bad, there's no reliable way for conda to "guess" what it should be.  The only thing conda can do is refuse to install it, but leave your current install the way it is.  That's what happened.

I looked at the conda.tgz file you uploaded, and the corrupt package is pretty apparent.  The contents of `pkgs/six-1.10.0-py35_0/info/files` is

```
lib/python3.5/site-packages/__pycache__/six.cpython-35.pyc
lib/python3.5/site-packages/six-1.10.0-py3.5.egg-info/PKG-INFO
lib/python3.5/site-packages/six-1.10.0-py3.5.egg-info/SOURCES.txt
lib/python3.5/site-packages/six-1.10.0-py3.5.egg-info/dependency_links.txt
lib/python3.5/site-packages/six-1.10.0-py3.5.egg-info/top_level.txt
lib/python3.5/site-packages/six.py
```

but in that package those four egg-info files don't exist, and `lib/python3.5/site-packages/six-1.10.0-py3.5.egg-info` is instead a file.

I just checked the package as it exists right now at https://repo.continuum.io/pkgs/free/osx-64/six-1.10.0-py35_0.tar.bz2.  It appears the package has been fixed, so you shouldn't have this problem with six again.

Conda *does* cache packages, and if @ilanschnell opts to update a package without bumping the build number, conda probably won't download the new package if it's already cached.  `conda clean --packages --tarballs` can help here, but if conda sees the package as "in use," it won't right now remove it from the cache.  `rm -rf $PREFIX/pkgs` is a perfectly good solution if you don't mind fresh downloads to populate the cache.

> What this means is that in order for it to coerce a string value of 'true'|'True' or 'false'|'False' into a true bool, I need to do it myself.

I'm sorry for bringing this up again (as I previously stated, I'm not really familiar with Voluptuous), but wouldn't this be fixed if the code called `Boolean()` instead of `All(Any(bool, int), Coerce(bool))`?

From the Boolean source code:
```python
def Boolean(v):
    """Convert human-readable boolean values to a bool.

    Accepted values are 1, true, yes, on, enable, and their negatives.
    Non-string values are cast to bool.

    >>> validate = Schema(Boolean())
    >>> validate(True)
    True
    >>> validate("1")
    True
    >>> validate("0")
    False
    >>> with raises(MultipleInvalid, "expected boolean"):
    ...   validate('moo')
    >>> try:
    ...  validate('moo')
    ... except MultipleInvalid as e:
    ...   assert isinstance(e.errors[0], BooleanInvalid)
    """
    if isinstance(v, basestring):
        v = v.lower()
        if v in ('1', 'true', 'yes', 'on', 'enable'):
            return True
        if v in ('0', 'false', 'no', 'off', 'disable'):
            return False
        raise ValueError
    return bool(v)
```

This seems to be exactly what you are looking for, isn't it?

I'm guessing that the auth error is because you're sending an empty snapshot which is not valid. In future versions this will be handled better, but at the moment if you don't send snapshot unless it's actually populated I think you'll see results that make more sense.

Another issue with this code is that list_blobs does not follow continuation tokens so you may be missing pages of results. This is again something that will be made automatic in newer versions, but for the moment you'll need to implement that code and pass the marker parameter.

include='snapshots' is the correct way to specify including snapshots, so if you've tried the two above steps and still are not seeing expected results, please update your code and we can keep debugging. That being said, we do have [a test](https://github.com/Azure/azure-storage-python/blob/master/tests/test_storage_blob.py#L1427) for this functionality. Note this test does not show how to follow continuation tokens, though there is a test nearby that does if you want to see.

The ARM implementation switches from a single synchronous response to a standard polled long-running operation at 20+ items (which that C# client didn't seem to take into account at first glance), and maxes out at 500 embedded requests. I don't have enough VMs laying around in my test account to poke at the limits for the instanceView use case that I care most about, but fetching instanceView for 10 VMs usually comes back in < 1s, which is a HUGE improvement over 10 additional round trips. 

I'm sure there are a number of other things this could be useful for, but the fact that it remains undocumented on ARM is definitely of concern. I'm leaning toward using it anyway for our new inventory plugin; perhaps with a switch or auto-fallback to the standard method on any unexpected failures.

> I suspect the parser should be more complicated.
>
> The header field value can be
>    defined in ABNF syntax as:
>
>        Forwarded   = 1#forwarded-element
>
>        forwarded-element =
>            [ forwarded-pair ] *( ";" [ forwarded-pair ] )
>
>        forwarded-pair = token "=" value
>        value          = token / quoted-string
>
>        token = <Defined in [RFC7230], Section 3.2.6>
>        quoted-string = <Defined in [RFC7230], Section 3.2.6>
>
> It means the forwarded-pair elements could be in random order: by=a; host=
> b.com and host=b.com, by=a are both valid combinations.
> Also value could be optionally quoted, aiohttp shoud unquote it
> automatically.
> pair-name is case-insensitive: all 'Host', 'host', and 'HOst` are valid
> names:
>
>        Forwarded: for="_gazonk"
>        Forwarded: For="[2001:db8:cafe::17]:4711"
>        Forwarded: for=192.0.2.60;proto=http;by=203.0.113.43
>        Forwarded: for=192.0.2.43, for=198.51.100.17
>
>    Note that as ":" and "[]" are not valid characters in "token", IPv6
>    addresses are written as "quoted-string".
>

aiohttp response objects are context managers.

In older versions of aiohttp, the context managers were built in a way to be compatible with older versions of python along with new.

However, that also means that if you don't use the response object properly as a context manager, it throws an error.

We should use this like a context manager even if we don't care about being compatible with older versions of aiohttp.

For reference, here is my custom collector plugin for reading timestamps from a file:

```
class CustomPlugin(FileNameCollectorPlugin):
    def collect(self, submission_file):
        if os.path.basename(submission_file) == "timestamp.txt":
            regex = ('.*/'
                '(?P<student_id>[^/]+)/'
                '(?P<file_id>\w+)')
            info = re.match(regex, submission_file).groupdict()
            with open(submission_file, "r") as fh:
                timestamp = datetime.strptime(fh.read()[:-3], "%Y%m%d%H%M%S")
            info['timestamp'] = timestamp.isoformat(' ')
        else:
            info = super(CustomPlugin, self).collect(submission_file)

        return info
```

I'd use authentication tokens for this. HubAuth did recently get support for API tokens in the Authorization header, not just cookies. You can store these in a file once generated. I think we do need to have a page for requesting a new token in the Hub UI to complete the loop, though. It would look like:

1. request an API token (this is fiddly right now, but I'll add a page for it)
2. save that somewhere like `~/.nbgrader/token`
3. CLI apps look for this file, use it in Authorization header. If not present, point to Hub page where they can get one.

Spawners could request and install this token at launch, to make it easy to do it from the single-user-server terminal.

I think stdout/err redirection is generally preferable, as it's more robust to outputs that might be written directly to stdout/stderr in exceptional circumstances (crashes, etc.) or by subprocesses, which logging doesn't capture. All daemon tools I'm familiar with support redirecting stdout/stderr to log files, and I would generally recommend using this instead of modifying Python logging destinations, as it works not just for Jupyter, but all programs.

We solved this issue in the Atlas system.  A correct Atlas node stores 100% of the zonefile state.  Node operators are incentivized to host the 100% replica since it accelerates their lookups and ensures that they have the requisite state in the event of a network partition or a DoS attack on an upstream node.  The only case where a non-malicious node operator would want only a partial replica is when the node operator knows in advance which names will be queried, and can get away with not hosting the entire working set.

I'm going to go ahead and close this issue; if we want to re-visit storage incentives in the Atlas system, please re-open a new issue.

I would argue that if you think you want a `@hook` handler that runs for every hook, you're probably thinking about the problem wrong.  That is to say, I agree with your premise that you shouldn't restrict your logic to specific hooks, but I think you're not taking it quite far enough; I think that charm layers should be avoiding the use of `@hook` as much as possible and instead relying on states to drive behavior, and if you do have a `@hook` handler, the *only* thing it should be doing is setting one or more states (with the possible exception of install, but even that in many charms should be handled by a runtime layer).  In other words, you can think of the reactive dispatch loop as the single general hook that you mentioned.

For your example, I would suggest not handling the config-changed hook explicitly at all, and instead having the state handlers do the work and drive their behavior off of the config values, whether they were just changed or not.

@stub42 

I think this would be very useful. There is also a very common task of sending connection string data to form a URL at the destination. So, for a particular endpoint, port information and protocol information is useful:

<protocol>://<hostname>:<port>/<L7-path>

Some relation endpoints may expose multiple <hostname>:<port> pairs with a different protocol, e.g.:

- http and https endpoints;
- ldap, ldap + StartTLS, ldaps endpoints.

It seems like we need a generic API to provide endpoint connection string data over a relation so that it is not reinvented in every charm. This would make endpoints very useful in my mind.

Part of this is also always sending hostnames (whether retrieved via gethostname(2) or set manually for virtual hosting scenarios) as TLS is a very common scenario. Generally, certificates are generated with hostnames in the Subject and SAN fields which makes hostname usage in connection strings mandatory for a successful hostname validation.

There is an alternative to sending hostnames in using reverse DNS lookups on the receiving side but this will work only if the DNS infrastructure is set up properly or /etc/hosts is populated on all related units.

>  I was thinking of using `upgrade.series.started` for the `pre-series-upgrade hook` and `upgrade.series.completed` for `post-series-upgrade`.

I'm not familiar with these hooks. Is there and documentation about these? Those flags sound good to me.

*As a slight sidenote: it would be very useful if the Charm had to explicitly state "ok, everything is in order, it's we can continue with the series upgrade". This would translate to a reactive flag that a handler has to set in order for the series upgrade to progress. This would allow charms to go into "blocked" state, for example, if it needed specific resources for the series upgrade, certain config, or other stuff. Without such a mechanism, we might get into a situation where a series upgrade silently fails because the `pre-series-upgrade` handlers were waiting for the right environment (their preconditions never matched during the pre-series-upgrade hook).*

> `upgrade.charm.completed` is actually more accurate than `upgrade-charm.requested` since by the time that flag is set, the charm code and wheelhouse deps have actually already been updated. Thoughts?

This is true, but I think it will be confusing for people who haven't read your explanation.

I think most users think of the `upgrade-charm` hook as doing two things:

1. Upgrading the Charm
2. Upgrading the application

In that sense, when the first handler runs, 1. has already happened, so now only the application needs to be upgraded.

So what about `upgrade.application.requested`?

@simonklb One significant difference between dependencies and trigger is that with dependencies, if you clear a dependency it doesn't clear the dependents so when the dependency gets set again, an entire chain of behavior might be skipped.  For instance, if `installed` is cleared but `configured` and `started` aren't, then re-doing the install could skip configuring and starting the service.  Triggers avoid this automatically.

One solution I have thought (borrowed from some other system which I don't recall right now) is that each worker can infer which tests should be collected based on their id and total number of workers. This can be accomplished easily today because each worker knows its own id and knows how many workers there are in total (based on `PYTEST_XDIST_WORKER_COUNT` env variable), so they can implement `pytest_collect_ignore` to ignore every path except the Nth path that is given to it (assuming all workers receive the paths in the same order).

For example, here's a list of tests in a suite and which worker collects that file (with 3 workers):

```
tests/test_1.py  # gw0
tests/test_2.py  # gw1
tests/test_3.py  # gw2
tests/test_4.py  # gw0
tests/test_5.py  # gw1
tests/test_6.py  # gw2
...
```

And so on. 

With that working, the `master` node can then just say to all workers: "hey, run all the tests you have collected" and that's it.

This is of course a quick draft, just throwing this here to see what you guys think.

This gives you a pretty quick run-through: https://git-scm.com/book/en/v2/Git-Tools-Rewriting-History#Changing-Multiple-Commit-Messages

Generally, what we do is do a `git log --decorate --oneline` and find the commit farthest back that we want to squash (this means only the changes we've made locally in our fork and haven't been merged yet). We copy the hash of that first commit when we started working on the feature and do `git rebase -i <copied_hash>`. This brings us into interactive mode where we can pick, edit, fixup etc.... And remember, if you remove a line during interactive rebasing, it will pull that commit completely out of the rebase (generally no good, unless you meant to do it).

*  Similarly, restarting from a blank slate, I create a certificate where the roles are reversed.  That is, the CN is ``mail.example.com``, while ``www.example.com`` is mentioned as a SubjectAltName.  I assume this is easy and routine.  Call this certificate B.

1.  Suppose that rather than restarting from a clean slate, I want to create certificate A **and** certificate B, so that both exist and are usable at the same time. topic = 'test.py'

def log(message):

def run():
    c = Consumer(**conf)
    c.subscribe([topic])
    try:
        while True:
            message = c.poll(1.0)
            if message is None:
                log('poll timeout')
            elif message.error():
                log('Error: {}'.format(message.error()))
            else:
                log('Message: {}'.format(message.value()))
                time.sleep(2)
    except KeyboardInterrupt:
        pass
    c.close()

workers = []
for _ in range(2):
    w = Process(target=run)
    w.start()
    workers.append(w)
time.sleep(2)

p = Producer(**conf)
for _ in range(5):
    p.produce(topic, str(uuid4()))
    p.poll(0)
p.flush()

for w in workers:
    w.join()

I was hoping that you would reconsider #264.  The problem with that solution is that it violates the fundamental invariant:  t.astimezone(tz) is tz:
```python
>>> dateutil.__version__
'2.6.0a0'
>>> LA = dateutil.tz.gettz('US/Pacific')
>>> u = datetime.now(timezone.utc)
>>> u.astimezone(LA).tzinfo is LA
False
```
Furthermore, it is incompatible with PEP 495 which treats repeated times as equal 

```python
>>> u = datetime(2014, 11, 2, 8, tzinfo=timezone.utc)
>>> t0 = u.astimezone(LA)
>>> t1 = (u + timedelta(hours=1)).astimezone(LA)
>>> t0 == t1
False
```
One may think the #264 behavior is better than PEP 495, but it is really not.  Consider, for example
```
>>> t0 + timedelta(hours=1) == t1
False
```

It seems to me that your solution is somewhere in between of the pytz and PEP 495 approaches.  It would be really unfortunate to have three slightly incompatible solutions to the same problem going forward.

With respect to  PEP-495 datetime objects as a backport, this is a possibility, but I think it is an overkill for the purposes of just fixing this dateutil bug.  I think most people would prefer using dateutil with the official datetime module.

In any case, assuming a backport of PEP 495 or Python 3.6 datetime module is available, how should dateutil use it?  Would you really want to change program behavior depending on whether  PEP 495 implementation is available?

To start with we can switch it over to using the fast path, since that one is not a behavior change.

Maybe we can add a public method `validate_rule()` that fires as part of the constructor to validate the rule and throw an exception at construction-time if it's invalid. Downstream users can subclass and override that method if they want the old behavior.

+1 for seeing the attempted bugfix as a breaking change.  In Australia, our standard formats would be either DDMMYYYY or YYYYMMDD (ISO format) - and dateutil used to read those both correctly with dayfirst set to true and yearfirst not explicitly set.  According to the table in issue #238, there's now no way to configure this behaviour.  We're trying to move from debian jessie to stretch - I guess we'll just pin stretch to the jessie version of dateutil, until you have time to provide an interface that allows these preferences to be specified. @Doron-Behar Do not forget that tmux itself is a terminal emulator. If you want to test performance of gnome-terminal rendering vs … rendering you need to test it without tmux. Otherwise you most likely are testing tmux performance summed with terminal emulator performance. E.g. when I print `dmesg` in a konsole window I get next prompt displayed immediately. When I do the same in tmux I am able to catch something like 3 frames of intermediate state.

Also note that tmux has one server and, according to pstree, it looks like the server has to process *all* output of *all* applications run inside while being itself single-threaded. I do not know where that output is rendered by tmux (it e.g. could simply store it and push to client to do actual rendering), but even concurrently reading applications’ output in a single-threaded single-process application may be a challenging task when there are many applications.

Hi Yechen Qiao,

This is a known discrepancy, which we should have, at minimum, documented clearly and did not. The cycle-based metrics in the summary chart, e.g. error, q30, yield, are computed over “useable cycles”. A usable cycle is one that is fully corrected in terms of phasing/prephasing. We don’t consider the last cycle of a run as usable, because it is not fully corrected. So, we don’t count this cycle toward yield or % >= q30 or error rate. Many aligners drop the last cycle for this reason.

The analysis chart shows all cycles for every plot (otherwise we introduce a new inconsistency between plots). The dumptext algorithm also provides data for all cycles, which coincides well with its purpose.

This is mostly addressed here in the InterOp documentation: http://illumina.github.io/interop/calculated_metrics.html

We will work to better address this issue in the documentation.

Best,
Rob

It's not intuitive for me either, having `RecurrentLayer` derive from `MergeLayer` sounds awkward. But it's a layer that requires input not only from the layer directly preceding it, but also from another `InputLayer`, so making it a `MergeLayer` seems like the cleanest solution. Unlike the `LSTMLayer` with its hidden and cell state, a `RecurrentLayer` is not actually producing multiple outputs, it's just consuming multiple inputs.

> than the multiplex layer idea.

Note that it could just as well be called `combine` and `pick` instead of `Multiplex` and `Demultiplex`, if that makes it any more intuitive. With that solution, a `RecurrentLayer` would produce multiple outputs (even if one of them is actually just passed on unchanged), and consume multiple inputs without being a `MergeLayer`.

I don't know if this makes sense. If you write data with a given schema then it should always be read with that schema. The only use-case I could see is that when you want to convert data from schema A to schema B, but I think that use-case needs to be handled differently. In particular, how do you know which fields in schema A map to schema B and vice-versa without having additional information about how the two schema map to each other?

@ajtritt I thought about several options last night, and the only one I came up with that is not terrible is illustrated in https://github.com/nicain/pynwb/tree/issue/constructor_arg

Basically the decorator contructor_arg gets special treatment, and moved out of the metaclassing in DecExtenderMeta (not illustrated in the branch, but pulled out of __prepare__ and made a function at module level).  

The advantage of this is that it is explicit about what is being done (tagging these methods with particular information), and works in python2 and python3.  The disadvantages are that it decouples what the information is used for away.  Also, its a pretty serious change to the design of how all the classes in build/map are built, so I am not pushing this as a solution.  Its just the best option I could come up with, out of several bad ones.

Let me know what you think about this, or if you have other suggestions.

As an additional note, I'm worried about your motivations for using angr. In general, angr is a very technical tool, not suited for beginners to binary analysis. For example, your question about the meaning of "sub_0x400390" makes us (the angr team) a little wary about holding your hand through this whole process - the answer to that question is "it's the subroutine at address 0x400390, which is technically not part of the main binary, but an address in angr's externs object, which provides addresses for unresolved imported symbols." If this answer goes entirely over your head, you may want to reconsider using angr...

@andyprata What you see is the result of not resampling actually :)

What happens is that the different bands from Modis are read in their original resolution, but can't be combined into a truecolor composite due to, well, having different resolutions. So satpy delays the creation of the composite, with hope that the user will resample the scene. When the resampling is done, the composite would be created. However, in your case, no resampling is performed, so at the save stage, the truecolor composite can't be found...

Hi Raja,

I suspect this is a misunderstanding between threads in Python and the 
pthreads in the underlying system.

When you start python, it runs as a single process with a single 
pthread. This is due to design choices in Python that makes it single 
threaded by nature as seen from the operating system. Thus everything 
will run on the same core.

The only way around this in Python is to spawn off new Python processes 
where you then communicate through pipes or something similar. This is 
done for the communication with Dirac (one for the interactive commands, 
one for the monitoring) and that is probably why you see three cores active.

The NumWorkerThreads controls the number of Python threads. This is 
useful to increase if there is a limit due to waiting for authentication 
from a server etc, as it can then use the local core more efficiently.

Cheers,
         Ulrik.

Hi, everyone.
I've managed to struggle with the same issue for the last couple of ours.
The problem is accessing a localhost from a docker container.
According to docker docs:
"The host has a changing IP address (or none if you have no network access). From 18.03 onwards our recommendation is to connect to the special DNS name host.docker.internal, which resolves to the internal IP address used by the host.
The gateway is also reachable as gateway.docker.internal."
https://docs.docker.com/docker-for-windows/networking/#per-container-ip-addressing-is-not-possible
So the solution in this case is to change the host name:
from
`function main(splash)
  splash:on_request(function(request)
    request:set_proxy{
        host = "127.0.0.1",
        port = 24000,
    }
  end)`
to
`function main(splash)
  splash:on_request(function(request)
    request:set_proxy{
        host = "host.docker.internal",
        port = 24000,
    }
  end)`

Only the following F200 pixel formats are required for F200 to operate correctly:
0x49564e49-... <=> INVI  - Infrared
0x495a4e49-... <=>INZI   - Depth + Ir
0x5a564e49-... <=> INVZ - Depth
These three formats are covered by Ubuntu 16 script which is  called patch-uvcvideo-16.04.simple.sh
The specific format that you mentioned, 0x494c4552, is not supported and therefore is not in the patch.

Check that the three above formats are resolved, i.e. not on dmesg's "black list".

Check field "Name":

Your understanding is correct. Each RealSense device has an internal hardware clock that can be used to understand the time difference between frames arriving from separate streams (depth, color, etc.) on the same device, and we abstract over this to provide frame timestamps that are coherent for that device.

Unfortunately, we do not have a clear mechanism to synchronize the timing between two independent RealSense devices viewing the same scene. Ideally you would want both devices consuming the same hardware clock signal, or you could calibrate after the fact if the mutually visible scene contained some known source of timing information, such as a blinking light or physical clock, and we can't make either assumption at the moment.

Inside align processing block we map each depth pixel to a rectangle in the target image (see [align.cpp:30-44](https://github.com/IntelRealSense/librealsense/blob/master/src/proc/align.cpp#L30-L44) to create image without black pixels. This is pretty much equivalent to NEAREST interpolation, and it does not destroy depth quality.

I'm not opposed in principle to changing the order of the palette. Any order that lists orange first and grey last is fine with me.

**However,** this is not a robust solution. You want runs that appear nearby _in the plot_ to have different colors; differentiating colors among runs with lexicographically similar _names_ will not accomplish this. For instance, if you have the runs
```
lr=1e-3,conv=1,fc=1
lr=1e-3,conv=1,fc=2
lr=1e-3,conv=2,fc=1
lr=1e-3,conv=2,fc=2
```
and you use the [orange, blue, red, cyan] order, then `conv=1,fc=1` and `conv=2,fc=1` will have similar colors; if those runs have similar curves (which seems plausible), then you'd have the same problem that you see here.

In general, there is **no universal ordering** that will work well on all datasets.

Note also that we are actually using Paul's suggested order: he says,
> I give the colours in order of hue, but if you prefer a 'random' order
> that still looks ordered: orange, blue, red, cyan, magenta, teal, grey.

We list the colors in order of hue, which is his primary suggestion. The order that you listed is his secondary suggestion, not his "suggested order."

I don't see how #86 is related.

Historically, `String8` was used for both text strings and binary data, as the code was Python 2 only. Now that Python 3 is also supported, we need to differentiate those 2 cases; in particular, we want proper Unicode strings for text data.

When not explicitly specified (see https://github.com/python-xlib/python-xlib/pull/52#issue-168806762), X11 text strings should be using the Host Portable Character Encoding (basically ASCII). So I agree that the fact that on Python 3 we end up using UTF-8 is not right, but neither would using Latin-1. Instead I think we should be using ASCII, as in Python 2, so we can detect all invalid use of  `String8` for binary data.

Your embedding matrix will looks like this (based on your example):
```
[[0.0    0.0   0.0  ]  <--- needed for consistency (but it will not be used, as id '0' will be masked)
 [0.4   -0.2   0.04]  <--- food
 [0.97 1.23  -2.3 ]  <--- is
 [1.45 -2.34 0.23]  <--- caught
 [ ........               ]  <--- A last id for the unknown words
```
Where ids are defined as follow:
```
food -> 1
is -> 2
caught -> 3
```
Then you can used id 0 as a masking value (to be used with dynamical RNNs, see the example).
Then the sentence 'food is really caught' will be generated as [1 2 4 3].

'params' is your matrix (above) and 'ids' is your incoming tensor.

@kmvanbrunt I thought I should share what I really like about the default_to_shell flag. The cmd2 app I've been working on involves interacting with a remote API as well as the local filesystem a lot. I wrote a `do_cd` command to call `os.chdir`, and added a call to `os.getcwd()` when my prompt is generated. Users can type cd, ls, cat, tail, etc, just as they can in a normal shell.

I think it's nice that users do not have to retrain their muscle memory to add an `!` to these extremely common commands. I understand that one can reimplement this behavior in their own application instead of inside of cmd2, but just how *easy* it was to get this behavior is one of the reasons I chose cmd2 over others. The setup it took to get a shell with the features I wanted was so minimal.

Basically, you have to have at least one virtual method in order to have a proper polymorphic class.  In practice, that's almost always the case anyway because you almost always want to have a virtual destructor (`virtual ~A() = default;`) in your base class, and *especially* when using pybind11: we're holding the object in an `A` pointer, and destroying through that pointer which is Undefined Behaviour if you don't have a virtual destructor.

No, my idea would be to offer an alternative to DNS challenge.

For a wildcard setup, you need to prove control over all the subdomains. The only obvious way to do this is to prove technical ownership of the corresponding DNS zone.

Now I’m wondering whether there could be another way more like the other challenges types. Asking random subdomain would prove that you have control over a wildcard redirection. I don’t know whether that would be enough for the IETF, I guess they have thought about it and there must be some issues.

For instance I’m wondering whether this case is possible:
– somespecificsub.domain.com points towards a given IP;
– *.domain.com and domain.com points towards another IP.

In that case, you could answer requests for the main domain and any subdomain excepted somespecificsub.domain.com. And I think it wouldn’t be right to deliver you a *.domain.com cert. So if that is possible, then we would need some additional setup. Like a permanent TXT record telling that it is authorized to do random subdomain verification for the wildcard challenge response. That way DNS configuration would just be fire-and-forget, and you could more easily validates a wildcard.

Anyway, I don’t know with who this should be discussed and at which point they have considered this setup, neither what are the exact required criterion for delivering a wildcard securely. I guess I should just read the RFC for that, but I don’t have time for this.

Some notes on existing `IDisplay` methods:

* `~certbot.interfaces.IDisplay.notification`'s `pause` arg is like a gentle version of the way `~certbot.interfaces.IDisplay.yesno` is sometimes used and it's not entirely clear when one is used instead of the other
* `~certbot.interfaces.IDisplay.menu`'s `ok_label`, `cancel_label`, and `help_label` args are unused
* `~certbot.interfaces.IDisplay.menu`'s ability to take tuples as choices is only used one place (and when it's used, we don't accept the "key" as valid user input, just the number, which seems... wrong)
* `~certbot.interfaces.IDisplay.yesno`'s `yes_label` and `no_label` args are only used in one place
* `~certbot.interfaces.IDisplay.checklist`'s `default_state` is unused (and `FileDisplay.checklist` uses a different argument, `default_status`, anyway)
* `~certbot.interfaces.IDisplay.directory_select` allows for selecting any path, not just a directory (and is used for selecting files)

A general observation is that it seems like many of Certbot's have one-off functionality. While there are advantages to tailoring the specifics of each user interaction to best fit the use case, it seems like there are disadvantages to doing so, such as inconsistent UX between workflows and increasing complexity in the shared display code.

> Will they have to modify setup.py downstream to prevent Certbot erroring out with a message about our argparse dependency not being satisfied?

It depends on whether their distro's Python packaging tooling automatically emits a dependency on a nonexistent argparse distro package. If it does, then yes, they'll have to tell it not to. At least in the case of debs, that's not a big deal and can be done one time (by including a patchfile against setup.py) and apply automatically thereafter.

The Certbot package on Debian based distros contains both a cronjob and systemd timer. It is set up in both places to help ensure renewal occurs if cron or systemd is disabled or isn't working properly.

The cronjob is a noop when systemd is used though. If you look at the cron command being run, it starts with:
```
test -x /usr/bin/certbot -a \! -d /run/systemd/system
```
The latter part of that checks if `/run/systemd/system` exists and if it does, it exits without running Certbot.

You can verify this by looking at Certbot's logs in `/var/log/letsencrypt`.
