I would say that the error is not triggered in lines 1-3, but exactly in the fourth:

```
subprocess.call(['sudo', 'cp',self.rules_local_path, self.rules_system_path])
```
Indeed, ensuring that the directoy actually exists, makes no difference. You can check it yourself.

> About the sudo, I think this solution can be used to cover non-sudo systems: 'cmd 2>/dev/null || sudo cmd'.

Won't `apio drivers --enable 2>/dev/null || sudo apio drivers --enable` execute the exact same code? I.e., `subprocess.call(['sudo', 'cp', self.rules_local_path, self.rules_system_path])`

> I don't think it is a good idea to configure the drivers in a docker container. May be the configuration must be done in the main system to enable FTDI comms. If you succeed with this, let me know.

An interesting feature of docker images is that a command (or sequence of commands) can be executed as soon as a container is started. Therefore, the content in `/etc/udev/rules.d` can be copied from the container to the host and execute then `service udev restart`.

Furthermore, I'd say that `service udev restart` is biased to Ubuntu systems. E.g., in latest Arch/Fedora releases `systemctl restart systemd-udevd.service` would be the preferred command: http://man7.org/linux/man-pages/man8/systemd-udevd.service.8.html Then, allowing to avoid it's execution (or automatically disabling it if a certain environment variable is set), would help to move that step in order to be executed later.

From what I've seen in the docuemntation there is either an NFC or I2C session that uses the same APDU commands to read/write NDEF content. Thus it appears possible to drive the chip via I2C as if it would be a discovered Type 4A Tag. To implement such, the driver needs to return the basic tag discovery data from the sense() method after opening an I2C session with the chip and implement the exchange() method to send and receive raw data. With that in place, the nfcpy TT4 implementation would behave as if a tag was found and produce the APDU commands for interaction.
The whole flow of commands can be observed from using a standard reader device and reading/writing the tag via NFC. The debug log would show all the APDU exchanges that would later be directly send through I2C.

I have implemented a very simple snippet that makes a parameter always check execution. 

Could someone (most likely @alexcjohnson ) let me know what you think of this implementation? 

```python
class HandshakeParameter(StandardParameter):
    """
    If a string is specified as a set command it will append '*OPC?' and use
    instrument.ask instead of instrument.write
    """
    def _set_set(self, set_cmd, set_parser):
        exec_str = self._instrument.ask if self._instrument else None
        if isinstance(set_cmd, str):
            set_cmd += '\n*OPC?'
        self._set = Command(arg_count=1, cmd=set_cmd, exec_str=exec_str,
                            input_parser=set_parser, no_cmd_function=no_setter)

        self.has_set = set_cmd is not None
```

I don't like this implementation because it will **always** verify commands, leading to a potential slowdown. However it does solve the immediate problem in a "nice" way.

Is there any configuration key to set to persist `id_token` in addition to basic info, and avoid persisting **all extra data**?

In general: 
- Without `extra_data` the following info are persisted in the database: 

      'token_type', 'access_token', 'expires', 'refresh_token', 'auth_time'

- Setting `SOCIAL_AUTH_GOOGLE_OPENIDCONNECT_GET_ALL_EXTRA_DATA=True`, the following info are persisted in the database:

      'token_type', 'access_token', 'expires_in', 'profile', 'family_name', 'kind',
      'gender', 'sub', 'id_token', 'picture', 'email_verified', 'locale', 'given_name',
      'auth_time', 'email', 'name'
  Note that the `refresh_token` is removed!

It seems `EXTRA_DATA` is the correct property to focus on:
https://github.com/python-social-auth/social-core/blob/a5d250e14a38a61e8fe97493b0061ad5b1457090/social_core/backends/open_id_connect.py#L34


So, I did the following which seems to be doing the trick:

```python
backend.EXTRA_DATA.append('id_token')
backend.EXTRA_DATA.append('refresh_token')
```

How safe/correct is this method?

Ok, thanks for the clarifications, it makes a lot of sense.

Though Ray doesn't currently provide a fine-grained status API, I think you could meet a lot of the requirements by tracking the scheduling state at the application level, e.g. keeping a registry of created actors, what tasks they're currently executing, etc. You could feed then feed this state to your systems that manage resource allocation. For example, we use a counter to track the resources used by currently active actors in the `ray.tune` app for admission control: https://github.com/ray-project/ray/blob/master/python/ray/tune/trial_runner.py#L200

The only thing that may be a fundamental limitation is the fractional GPUs thing, which prevents actors from sharing GPUs.

Streamlink is designed to handle one video stream at a time, it streams the video content to the player via a pipe (or HTTP). As far as the player is concerned the video that it is playing is a single video stream, ie. if you want to play more than video stream back-to-back they need to be concatenated. Streamlink has no facility to supply multiple separate video streams to a player.

If you hacked `handle_url` it would spawn one instance of the player for each video that you played.

This *cannot* be reliably done with a role without a server's permissions being designed for this usage. 

If this makes it into core, it needs to error in cases it would be ineffective with a clear reason why presented to the user.

Discord's developer docs on permissions explain this, but not really in good user-facing terms. If a user has any role allowing something (other than the default role), any role-based denies won't matter since non-default role allows are batch applied *after* batch applying non-default role denies.

This ends up making it so that channels which are invisible to most, visible but mute to role B and non-mute to role C can't be effectively muted without also revoking read permissions without assigning permissions granularly, and ensuring members of C are also members of B. 

It gets significantly more convoluted with more restricted channels. 

I'm of the opinion it's worth supporting, but given the various caveats for it, it needs to be opt-in, with well-documented reasons as to why, and how to properly configure it, and warn users if it isn't going to work on someone they attempt to mute with it.


to

```javascript
for (var _i = 0, state_1 = state; _i < state_1.length; _i++) { }
```

Ah, Windows is an interesting point. AFAICT Windows doesn't have any equivalent of catching SIGTERM; for ordinary process termination there's just `TerminateProcess`, and that's the equivalent of `SIGKILL`. But, they do have ["services"](https://msdn.microsoft.com/en-us/library/windows/desktop/ms685141(v=vs.85).aspx), which are the equivalent of Unix daemons, and the service API does have a way for a "service manager" (their equivalent or runit or supervisord or whatever) to [send a request to a service to exit cleanly](https://msdn.microsoft.com/en-us/library/windows/desktop/ms683241(v=vs.85).aspx).

So this is making me think that maybe what we really want here is some helpful high-level APIs for writing daemon-like programs, that make it easy to handle the three main cases of run in foreground, run daemonized, run as windows service, and abstract over things like SIGTERM versus SERVICE_CONTROL_STOP.

* https://twistedmatrix.com/trac/ticket/5705
* https://twistedmatrix.com/documents/current/core/howto/application.html

Oh hmm. The interaction between cancellation and blocking-`start` is complicated.

The simple case is simple: if we're just doing
```python
async with open_nursery() as nursery:
    await nursery.start(...)
```
then any cancellation that affects the call to `start` also affects the child equally. But in the general case, the nursery and the task calling `start` could be totally unrelated.

What if the call to `start` gets cancelled? What effect should this have on the child? Intuitively, we sort of want to think of the child as being supervised by the spawner until it calls `.started()`. This would argue that `start` being cancelled should propagate into the child (and any child that it's spawned in the meantime, etc.), and then `start` shouldn't return until it's exited. OTOH the naive implementation would just have cancelled `start` abandon the child to keep running (i.e. it turns into a `start_soon`). Right now we really don't have any good way to swap around the cancel scopes affecting a task...

Also, what if the nursery gets cancelled? What affect should this have on the spawner blocked in `start`? Generally, I think if the child crashes before calling `started`, then we want the exception to propagate out to the spawner. (There's some question about what exactly should happen with nurseries that do automatic restarting, but I guess we can worry about that later.) But if the nursery is cancelled, and throws `Cancelled` into the child, and that `Cancelled` propagates into the spawner... then suddenly we have this loose `Cancelled` that might refer to a totally different cancellation hierarchy, and nothing can catch it until it kills the whole program. This is really no good at all. One option would be to convert it into a `NurseryCancelled` exception or something like that when it crosses over to the spawner. That doesn't seem too bad – "sorry, spawning into this nursery didn't work, it's cancelled". Trying to spawn into a closed nursery raises a `RuntimeError` -- this is similar. Except... if the cancellation *does* encompass both the nursery and the spawner, then we really *should* keep it as a `Cancelled` error so that it can eventually be absorbed by a `move_on_after` or whatever, instead of converting it into a "real" error that might propagate out to crash the program. Hmm. The other option would be to say that the task simply isn't affected by the nursery's cancellation state until after it calls `started()`. This would be consistent with the concept suggested above, that before `started()` the child is a child of the spawner, and after `started()` it's a child of the nursery.

Maybe one way to do it would be to literally have an `adopt` method on nurseries, that can move a task from one nursery to another, and then do something like:
```python
@attr.s
class _TaskStatus:
    _final_nursery = attr.ib()
    _child = attr.ib(default=None)
    _spawner = attr.ib(default=None)

async def start(self, async_fn):
    # remember that 'self' here is the eventual nursery
    task_status = _TaskStatus(self)
    async with open_nursery() as nursery:
        task = nursery.start_soon(partial(async_fn, task_status=task_status))
        task_status._child = task
        return await task_status._wait()
```
where `task_status.started()` does `self._final_nursery.adopt(self._child)` to pull the child out from underneath the spawner.

Would this `adopt` method have any other uses? It's conceptually sound, but seems pretty weird. We could keep it internal for now in any case.

This approach would also fix something that's a little tricky with the current nursery interface: if the task exits/crashes before calling `.started()`, do we notify the nursery? We kind of have to right now, but this can cause an exception to be raised twice. This won't be an issue after we remove the notification APIs, like we're planning to do in #136, but I was sort of thinking that part could wait until 0.3.0, and I wanted to get `.start()` into 0.2.0.

This proposed semantics also makes it even more unclear what `.start` should do on a "proper" supervisor. If it's the supervisor who takes care of restarting, then like... it shouldn't do that until it's taken possession of the child, right? Conceptually? But we don't want an intermittent failure during startup to break the whole startup sequence; we probably do want `start` to retry failures following the normal logic. Maybe that's just fine and we shouldn't worry about the conceptual issue – fancy_supervisor.start() certainly *can* implement some fancy logic if it wants. In fact in general it might need to do something like, wait for the previous task to finish starting before it can even get going. But what should the cancellation semantics be then? Fancy supervisors probably have a way to cancel individual tasks so propagating from spawner→child is certainly doable. The other direction is less clear. But we certainly would need to do *something* if the supervisor guarantees that tasks will be started one at a time in serial, and then one of the precondition tasks never starts because it's cancelled. The strategy suggested above for plain nurseries of blithely letting the child continue on until it calls `startup()` and then dropping the cancellation on it -- that doesn't work at all.

And it does seem a bit odd to allow tasks to be blithely spun all the way up to "started" and report success to their spawner and then immediately be cancelled.

Hmm.

I think this is due to a bug in how SpanDatas are converted into the legacy trace json format.  [This line](https://github.com/census-instrumentation/opencensus-python/blob/master/opencensus/trace/span_data.py#L161) incorrectly assumes that all spans passed to it are part of the same trace.

We should either:
1. Update the exporters to use the Span Data objects directly, bypassing the legacy trace json format conversion
2. Group the spans in the exporter by trace id before formatting them to the legacy trace json format.

Not true, @duskybomb.  While I avoid spaces in filenames, there is generally a way to escape spaces when passing them to the command line.

The issue is with these 2 lines:

```
    convert = "convert -density 350 %s -depth 8 tiff:-" % (path)
    p1 = subprocess.Popen(convert.split(' '), stdout=subprocess.PIPE)
```

This is terrible code because the command is built as string. The correct way is to build it as list and pass it to `subprocess`. E.g. ([from](https://docs.python.org/3/library/subprocess.html) the official docs)

`subprocess.run(["ls", "-l"])`

There is another complication to this problem: How do you install Python in Git Bash? Msys, or Win32 native? The Win32 native Python can work correctly in Git Bash (it is just another Window program), and detects bash.exe correctly. But I don’t even know how to install Python in Msys…

In addition, this was related to attempting to using pipenv shell and having the WinError. Making this same correction in the shellingham used in Pipenv resolved the problem. There are subtle differences between the two. On Windows 7 32-bit, for example, in a virtualenv created with `--distribute`, running `easy_install` will always require UAC elevation (and subsequently have its output in a new command window), which kind of defeat the purpose of using a virtualenv. As the problem doesn't exist with the setuptools variant, I think setuptools is the safer choice for now.

By the way, on Windows 2008 R2 (which is 64-bit), both distribute and setuptools variants work fine for me. This is like a dark art.

    549     Write to disc
    550     >>> import tempfile, os
    551     >>> directory = tempfile.mkdtemp()
    552     >>> open(os.path.join(directory, 'test.ics'), 'wb').write(cal.to_ical())

This would be useful to have merged into virtualenv's mainline.  I also hit this bug and Satra's patch fixes things for me.  If you really need a copy of the EPD 64-bit for code validation purposes I can see about getting you a non-commercial license for your 64-bit machine.
